questions.exp: Fibrinogen plays a dual role: (1) as a clotting factor — it is cleaved by thrombin to form fibrin monomers that polymerise and are cross-linked to form a stable clot; and (2) as a positive acute phase protein — fibrinogen levels rise during inflammation and infection, contributing to rouleaux formation (RBCs stacking like coins), which elevates the erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). The ESR is thus an indirect measure of fibrinogen and acute phase response.

questions.q: A patient is described as having 'serum electrophoresis' for diagnosis. The test reveals a tall, narrow monoclonal band in the gamma globulin region (M-protein). What condition does this most likely indicate?
opts: A. Severe bacterial infection causing polyclonal expansion of all gamma globulin subtypes
B. Haemophilia A with elevated Factor VIII replacement antibodies
C. Multiple myeloma — a malignancy of plasma cells producing a single clone of identical immunoglobulins (monoclonal band)
D. Iron deficiency anaemia with compensatory increase in transferrin production
E. Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency with absent alpha globulin band
F. Chronic inflammatory disease with polyclonal hypergammaglobulinaemia
questions.exp: A monoclonal (M) band on serum protein electrophoresis — a tall, narrow spike in the gamma region — indicates a clonal plasma cell disorder such as multiple myeloma. Myeloma plasma cells are a single abnormal clone producing identical immunoglobulins (usually IgG or IgA). Infection causes a broad polyclonal increase (F). Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency shows absent alpha band (E). Iron deficiency does not produce a monoclonal band (D).

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes how the extrinsic pathway initiates coagulation?
opts: A. The extrinsic pathway begins when Factor XII contacts negatively charged surfaces, activating XI then IX
B. The extrinsic pathway begins when VWF exposes subendothelial collagen, activating platelets directly

C. The extrinsic pathway begins when blood contacts Tissue Factor (released from perivascular and subendothelial cells following vessel damage), which binds Factor VIIa — the TF-FVIIa complex then activates Factors IX and X
D. The extrinsic pathway begins with Factor XI activation, proceeding to IX then X
E. The extrinsic pathway begins with thrombomodulin activation of Protein C to initiate coagulation
F. The extrinsic pathway begins when endothelin from damaged endothelium activates Factor VII directly
questions.exp: The extrinsic pathway (Tissue Factor pathway) begins when Tissue Factor (TF) — a protein present in perivascular and subendothelial cells — is exposed by vessel damage and binds Factor VIIa in the blood. The resulting TF-FVIIa complex activates both Factor IX (shared with intrinsic pathway) and Factor X (entering the common pathway). This initiates coagulation rapidly (8-10 seconds). It is measured by the Prothrombin Time (PT).

questions.q: Your notes include a table of ABO plasma compatibility. If an AB positive patient requires a large volume plasma transfusion and AB plasma is unavailable, which plasma could safely be given?
opts: A. O negative plasma, as O is always the universal donor
B. Any AB plasma regardless of Rh type, as Rh does not affect plasma compatibility
C. Only AB negative plasma, as the Rh factor is critical in plasma
D. A positive or A negative plasma only
E. No alternative — only AB plasma is safe for AB recipients
F. Any plasma type — plasma compatibility is irrelevant as antibodies are too dilute to cause reactions
questions.exp: For plasma transfusion, compatibility is based on the ABO antibodies in the donor plasma (not the red cell antigens). AB plasma is ideal for AB recipients because AB individuals have no anti-A or anti-B antibodies — their plasma won't attack any recipient's red cells. When AB plasma is unavailable, any AB plasma (AB+ or AB-) is safe. Note: Rh factor is expressed on red cells, not plasma — so Rh type is irrelevant for plasma transfusion.

questions.q: The proerythroblast stage of erythropoiesis has a nucleus. At which subsequent stage is the nucleus lost, and what is the functional significance of this?
opts: A. The nucleus is lost at the erythroblast stage, allowing haemoglobin production to begin
B. The nucleus is never completely lost — mature erythrocytes retain a condensed nucleus
C. The nucleus is lost at the reticulocyte stage when immature RBCs lose their nuclei and move into the bloodstream, allowing more space for haemoglobin and preventing further division
D. The nucleus is lost at the erythrocyte stage inside the bone marrow before the cell is released
E. The nucleus is expelled at the proerythroblast stage to make room for haemoglobin synthesis
F. The nucleus is retained but becomes non-functional, giving the appearance of an anucleate cell
questions.exp: The reticulocyte is the stage at which immature RBCs lose their nuclei and enter the bloodstream. The nucleus is retained through proerythroblast (needed for cell division and haemoglobin gene expression) and erythroblast stages. Loss of the nucleus at the reticulocyte stage maximises intracellular space for haemoglobin, prevents further cell division, and explains why mature RBCs cannot synthesise proteins or repair themselves — contributing to their fixed 120-day lifespan.

questions.q: Adaptive immunity is described as developing a 'faster and more effective response' upon re-exposure to a pathogen. What cellular mechanism underlies this immunological memory?
opts: A. Innate immune cells (neutrophils and NK cells) retain molecular memory of previous pathogens through epigenetic changes

B. Memory B-cells and memory T-cells are generated during the primary immune response and persist long-term, allowing a rapid, amplified anamnestic (secondary) response upon re-exposure to the same antigen
C. Antibody levels remain permanently elevated after every infection, providing continuous protection
D. The liver synthesises pathogen-specific acute phase proteins that are retained indefinitely
E. Plasma cells continuously produce antibodies throughout life regardless of re-exposure
F. NK cells undergo antigen-specific clonal expansion and produce memory cells for future responses
questions.exp: Immunological memory is mediated by long-lived memory B-cells and memory T-cells generated during the primary immune response. Upon re-exposure to the same antigen, these memory cells rapidly differentiate into effector cells, producing a faster (hours rather than days), larger, and more specific response — the anamnestic (secondary) response. This is the scientific basis of vaccination. Memory B-cells can differentiate into plasma cells producing high-affinity antibodies.

questions.q: Your notes state that the biconcave shape of RBCs 'increases SA for gas exchange efficiency.' What other physical property of RBCs aids oxygen transport?
opts: A. Their rigid structure prevents deformation that would reduce haemoglobin exposure to plasma oxygen
B. Their large size maximises the total haemoglobin content per cell
C. Their flexibility allows them to deform and traverse capillaries smaller than their diameter, maximising tissue delivery of oxygen
D. Their spherical shape minimises turbulence in arteries, improving laminar flow
E. Their negatively charged surface repels other RBCs preventing aggregation and sedimentation in vessels
F. Their ability to synthesise ATP via mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation powers active oxygen transport
questions.exp: Beyond the biconcave disc shape increasing surface area, the key additional property is flexibility/deformability. RBCs can deform to pass through capillaries as narrow as 3-5 μm (smaller than their 7-8 μm diameter), ensuring oxygen delivery to all tissues. This flexibility depends on the spectrin-actin cytoskeleton. Loss of flexibility (in sickle cell disease, spherocytosis) causes haemolysis. Your notes state RBCs are 'flexible: to fit through small blood vessels.'

questions.q: A 25-year-old Rh-negative man requires a blood transfusion after a traumatic accident. He receives 2 units of O positive blood before his blood group is identified. What is the primary clinical concern?
opts: A. Acute ABO haemolytic reaction as O positive contains anti-A antibodies that will attack his red cells

B. The patient will develop anti-D (anti-Rh) antibodies from sensitisation to the RhD antigen on the O positive cells, and future Rh-positive transfusions will cause haemolytic reactions; in a male this is a lesser concern than in a female of childbearing age
C. O positive blood causes no clinical problems in any recipient and the transfusion is entirely safe
D. The patient will develop anti-D antibodies that will immediately haemolyse the transfused O positive cells causing an acute haemolytic reaction
E. The primary concern is ABO mismatch as O positive plasma contains anti-A and anti-B
F. Immediate anaphylaxis is the primary risk from Rh incompatibility
questions.exp: The concern with giving Rh-positive blood to an Rh-negative individual is sensitisation: the patient's immune system may generate anti-D IgG antibodies against the RhD antigen on the transfused cells. This is a first exposure — the immediate haemolytic risk is low (first exposure generates IgM then switches to IgG; haemolysis occurs on re-exposure). In a male this matters less clinically, but any subsequent Rh-positive transfusion could cause haemolysis. In Rh-negative women of childbearing age this is critical (risk of haemolytic disease of the newborn).

questions.q: In your notes, which blood group system is described as having the property of being 'usually insignificant, but anti-M occasionally causes haemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN)'?
opts: A. Kell system
B. Duffy system
C. Kidd system
D. MNSs system
E. Lewis system
F. P1PK system
questions.exp: The MNSs blood group system is described in your notes as 'usually insignificant, but anti-M occasionally causes haemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN).' The Kell system is highly immunogenic (A). The Duffy system is a receptor for Plasmodium vivax (B). The Kidd system can cause severe delayed haemolytic reactions (C). Lewis, P1PK, and Lutheran (E, F) are described as 'rarely causing any significant problems.'

questions.q: What is the difference between the classical two-pathway model of coagulation and the cell-based model described in your notes?
opts: A. There is no difference — both models describe identical processes with different terminology

B. The classical model describes separate intrinsic and extrinsic pathways converging on Factor X; the cell-based model recognises that coagulation occurs on specific cell surfaces (not just in plasma) through initiation, amplification, and propagation phases — this better reflects in vivo physiology
C. The cell-based model replaces the intrinsic pathway entirely with the amplification phase
D. The classical model is the physiologically accurate one; the cell-based model is a theoretical construct used only for teaching
E. The cell-based model eliminates the concept of Tissue Factor and focuses on platelet surface reactions only
F. The cell-based model adds a fourth pathway to the intrinsic, extrinsic, and common pathways
questions.exp: The classical model describes two separate pathways (intrinsic and extrinsic) converging on Factor X — useful for understanding coagulation tests (PT vs aPTT). The cell-based model better reflects in vivo physiology: coagulation occurs on specific cell surfaces (tissue factor-bearing cells and platelet surfaces) through three phases — initiation (TF-bearing cells), amplification (thrombin activates platelets and cofactors), and propagation (massive thrombin generation on platelet surfaces). Your notes describe the cell-based model as 'what actually happens in the body.'

questions.q: A patient has recurrent episodes of thrombosis including a DVT and pulmonary embolism at age 32. Genetic testing reveals a deficiency of Protein C. How does this lead to a prothrombotic state?
opts: A. Protein C deficiency causes excessive fibrinolysis, paradoxically increasing clot formation as a compensatory response

B. Protein C normally inactivates Factors Va and VIIIa — deficiency means these amplification cofactors are not adequately inactivated, allowing excessive and prolonged thrombin generation and thrombosis
C. Protein C deficiency prevents TFPI from shutting down the extrinsic initiation phase
D. Protein C deficiency reduces thrombomodulin expression on endothelial cells
E. Protein C deficiency reduces antithrombin activity, allowing unregulated serine protease activity
F. Protein C deficiency causes platelet hyperactivation by failing to inhibit GpIIb/IIIa receptors
questions.exp: Protein C is activated by thrombomodulin-bound thrombin and then inactivates Factors Va and VIIIa — the key cofactors that amplify coagulation. Deficiency means Va and VIIIa are not adequately neutralised, allowing the amplification phase of coagulation to run unchecked, leading to excessive thrombin generation. This creates a hypercoagulable (prothrombotic) state — presenting as recurrent VTE at a young age. Protein C deficiency is an inherited thrombophilia.

questions.q: Your notes state that the Kidd blood group system 'can cause severe delayed reactions.' What type of transfusion reaction is this referring to?
opts: A. An acute haemolytic transfusion reaction occurring within minutes of the transfusion
B. An anaphylactic reaction to plasma proteins from the donor
C. A delayed haemolytic transfusion reaction occurring days after the transfusion when an anamnestic antibody response causes haemolysis of the transfused red cells
D. A febrile non-haemolytic transfusion reaction from donor white cell antigens
E. Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO) from excessive fluid administration
F. Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI) from donor antibodies attacking recipient neutrophils
questions.exp: Kidd (Jka, Jkb) antibodies are notorious for causing severe delayed haemolytic transfusion reactions — occurring days to weeks after transfusion. Kidd antibodies have a unique property of falling to undetectable levels between exposures (making pre-transfusion testing sometimes miss them), then rising rapidly on re-exposure in an anamnestic response — causing haemolysis of the transfused cells. This makes Kidd incompatibility particularly dangerous.

questions.q: In patients with significant liver disease, which of the following patterns on a coagulation screen would suggest hepatic synthetic dysfunction?
opts: A. Elevated PT only, with normal aPTT, as only Factor VII (extrinsic) is hepatically synthesised
B. Normal PT and aPTT with reduced platelet count — liver disease affects only platelets
C. Prolonged PT and aPTT, low fibrinogen, and low albumin — the liver synthesises most coagulation factors and albumin
D. Elevated aPTT only as heparin is metabolised by the liver and accumulates in liver disease
E. Elevated D-dimers only — liver disease causes fibrinolysis without affecting coagulation factor synthesis
F. Isolated thrombocytopenia from hypersplenism, with normal PT, aPTT, and fibrinogen
questions.exp: In significant hepatic synthetic dysfunction, the liver fails to produce adequate levels of coagulation factors (especially Factor VII with its 4-6 hour half-life — prolonging PT first, then aPTT as other factors fall) and fibrinogen (falling with severe disease). Albumin (exclusively hepatic synthesis) also falls. This pattern — prolonged PT > aPTT, low fibrinogen, low albumin — points to hepatic synthetic failure. Factor VIII is notably NOT liver-synthesised in significant amounts (produced by endothelium), so it may be normal or even elevated.

questions.q: Which of the following events during haemostasis represents the 'positive feedback' described in your notes regarding thrombin?
opts: A. Thrombin activates Protein C to shut down its own production — negative feedback preventing excessive clotting
B. Thrombin activates TFPI to inhibit further Tissue Factor pathway activity
C. A small amount of thrombin generated initially activates Factors V, VIII, and XI, amplifying the cascade to generate vastly more thrombin — positive feedback ensuring rapid clot formation
D. Thrombin activates antithrombin to neutralise excess serine proteases
E. Thrombin converts fibrinogen to fibrin which then inhibits further thrombin generation by sequestering it in the clot
F. Thrombin activates plasminogen to begin fibrinolysis as the clot is forming — simultaneous positive and negative feedback
questions.exp: Thrombin's positive feedback loop is a critical amplification mechanism: the small amount of thrombin initially generated (from TF-initiated extrinsic pathway) activates Factors V, VIII, and XI — all of which amplify further thrombin generation. This ensures that once clotting is initiated at a site of significant injury, the cascade rapidly generates massive amounts of thrombin sufficient to form a stable clot. This is the 'propagation' phase of the cell-based model. Thrombin also activates platelets, providing the phospholipid surface for further cascade amplification.

questions.q: A 70 kg male at rest has a heart rate of 75 bpm. His stroke volume is measured at 70 mL. What is his cardiac output?
opts: A. 4.5 L/min
B. 5.25 L/min
C. 6.0 L/min
D. 7.0 L/min
E. 3.5 L/min
questions.exp: CO = HR × SV = 75 × 70 mL = 5250 mL/min = 5.25 L/min. This is the normal mean resting cardiac output. Each ventricle produces this output independently, not the combined total of both ventricles.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly defines stroke volume?
opts: A. The total blood volume ejected by both ventricles per minute
B. The amount of blood ejected by each ventricle per contraction
C. The difference between maximum and resting cardiac output
D. The blood remaining in the ventricle after systole
E. The pressure difference between systole and diastole
questions.exp: Stroke volume is the volume of blood pumped by each ventricle per contraction. It is calculated as EDV − ESV. The normal range is 55–100 mL, with a mean of ~70 mL in a 70 kg individual.

questions.q: Which formula is used to calculate cardiac output?
opts: A. CO = SV − HR
B. CO = EDV − ESV
C. CO = HR × SV
D. CO = MAP / SVR
E. CO = SV / HR
questions.exp: CO = HR × SV. EDV − ESV gives stroke volume, not cardiac output. MAP / SVR is a rearrangement of Ohm's law for the cardiovascular system but does not directly define CO.

questions.q: A patient's echocardiogram shows an EDV of 130 mL and an ESV of 55 mL. What is the stroke volume?
opts: A. 185 mL
B. 130 mL
C. 75 mL
D. 55 mL
E. 60 mL
questions.exp: SV = EDV − ESV = 130 − 55 = 75 mL. EDV is the volume at the end of filling (diastole), and ESV is the volume remaining after ejection (systole). Their difference is the stroke volume.

questions.q: During vigorous exercise, a trained athlete's HR rises to 180 bpm and SV increases to 130 mL. What is their cardiac output?
opts: A. 15.6 L/min
B. 19.5 L/min
C. 23.4 L/min
D. 13.0 L/min
E. 9.1 L/min
questions.exp: CO = 180 × 130 = 23,400 mL/min = 23.4 L/min. Wait — re-check the options. The correct calculation is 180 × 130 = 23.4 L/min which matches C. The question stem states SV of 130 mL and HR of 180, giving 23.4 L/min.

questions.q: The cardiac reserve refers to which of the following?
opts: A. The residual volume of blood left in the ventricle after contraction
B. The difference between maximum and resting cardiac output
C. The ejection fraction at rest
D. The volume of blood stored in systemic veins
E. The additional heart rate achievable above resting
questions.exp: Cardiac reserve is the difference between maximum cardiac output (achieved during peak exercise) and resting cardiac output. In healthy individuals this is approximately 4–5× resting output; elite athletes can achieve 7–8× resting CO.

questions.q: Bradycardia is defined as a resting heart rate below which value?
opts: A. 50 bpm
B. 55 bpm
C. 60 bpm
D. 70 bpm
E. 100 bpm
questions.exp: Bradycardia is defined as a resting heart rate below 60 bpm. Tachycardia is defined as a resting rate above 100 bpm. The intrinsic SA node rate is approximately 100 bpm, but vagal tone normally keeps resting HR around 75 bpm.

questions.q: A 30-year-old patient has a resting HR of 110 bpm. Which term best describes this?
opts: A. Bradycardia
B. Normal sinus rhythm
C. Tachycardia
D. Cardiac reserve
E. Vagal tone
questions.exp: Tachycardia is defined as a resting heart rate above 100 bpm. This patient at 110 bpm meets that definition. Bradycardia is below 60 bpm. Normal sinus rhythm is 60–100 bpm.

questions.q: As heart rate rises significantly during exercise, stroke volume eventually decreases. What is the primary reason for this?
opts: A. Decreased sympathetic stimulation of the ventricles
B. Reduced venous return due to vasoconstriction
C. Less time in diastole reduces ventricular filling
D. Increased afterload from elevated blood pressure
E. Parasympathetic activation suppressing contractility
questions.exp: As HR increases, the diastolic interval shortens, leaving less time for the ventricles to fill. This reduces EDV and therefore stroke volume (SV = EDV − ESV). At very high HRs, the fall in SV is not compensated, so CO may actually decrease.

questions.q: The cardiovascular centres that regulate heart rate are located in which structure?
opts: A. Hypothalamus
B. Cerebral cortex
C. Medulla oblongata
D. Pons
E. Cerebellum
questions.exp: The cardiovascular centres are located in the medulla oblongata. The cardioaccelerator centre increases HR via sympathetic stimulation, while the cardioinhibitory centre decreases HR via the vagus nerve (parasympathetic).

questions.q: Which nerve carries parasympathetic signals from the cardioinhibitory centre to the heart?
opts: A. Phrenic nerve
B. Vagus nerve (CN X)
C. Glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX)
D. Facial nerve (CN VII)
E. Accessory nerve (CN XI)
questions.exp: The vagus nerve (cranial nerve X) carries parasympathetic fibres from the cardioinhibitory centre to the SA node, AV node, and portions of the atria and ventricles. Acetylcholine is released, slowing HR by opening K+ channels.

questions.q: Norepinephrine released by cardioaccelerator nerves binds to which receptor on cardiac muscle?
opts: A. Alpha-1 adrenergic receptor
B. Alpha-2 adrenergic receptor
C. Beta-1 adrenergic receptor
D. Beta-2 adrenergic receptor
E. Muscarinic receptor
questions.exp: Norepinephrine binds to beta-1 adrenergic receptors on the heart, opening Na+ and Ca2+ ligand-gated channels. This increases the rate of depolarisation, shortening repolarisation, and increasing HR and contractility. Beta-blockers target this receptor to slow HR.

questions.q: Acetylcholine released by the vagus nerve slows heart rate by which mechanism?
opts: A. Blocking calcium channels in the SA node
B. Opening ligand-gated potassium channels, slowing depolarisation
C. Stimulating alpha-1 receptors to reduce contractility
D. Closing sodium channels permanently
E. Increasing cAMP within cardiomyocytes
questions.exp: ACh opens ligand-gated K+ channels in the SA node, allowing K+ to flow out of the cell. This hyperpolarises the cell, slowing the rate of spontaneous depolarisation and extending the time between action potentials, thus decreasing HR.

questions.q: The intrinsic (autonomous) firing rate of the SA node is approximately what, before any autonomic modification?
opts: A. 60 bpm
B. 75 bpm
C. 100 bpm
D. 120 bpm
E. 150 bpm
questions.exp: The SA node fires intrinsically at ~100 bpm. Resting HR is ~75 bpm because vagal (parasympathetic) tone is predominant at rest, slowing it down. If both sympathetic and parasympathetic inputs are blocked, the heart defaults to ~100 bpm.

questions.q: Baroreceptors in the aortic sinus detect an increase in blood pressure. What is the expected cardiac response?
opts: A. Increased HR and increased SV
B. Decreased parasympathetic and increased sympathetic stimulation
C. Increased parasympathetic and decreased sympathetic stimulation
D. Release of epinephrine from the adrenal medulla
E. Constriction of arterioles to raise BP further
questions.exp: The baroreceptor reflex: increased stretch in the aortic sinus signals the cardiovascular centre to increase parasympathetic (vagal) and decrease sympathetic tone, slowing HR and reducing SV to lower blood pressure back to homeostatic levels.

questions.q: The Bainbridge (atrial) reflex is triggered by which stimulus?
opts: A. Decreased blood pressure in the carotid sinus
B. Increased venous return stretching the atrial walls
C. Chemoreceptors detecting low oxygen levels
D. Limbic system activation during stress
E. Decreased CO2 levels in arterial blood
questions.exp: The Bainbridge reflex: increased venous return stretches the atria, activating specialised atrial baroreceptors. The cardiac centre responds by suppressing parasympathetic stimulation and increasing HR, ensuring blood is pumped out of the heart more rapidly.

questions.q: A patient with hyperthyroidism (elevated T3/T4) develops a resting HR of 110 bpm. Which mechanism best explains this?
opts: A. T3 directly blocks potassium channels in the SA node
B. Thyroid hormones increase vagal tone
C. T3 enters cardiomyocytes and upregulates beta-adrenergic responsiveness
D. Thyroid hormones activate muscarinic receptors
E. T4 directly stimulates the cardioinhibitory centre
questions.exp: T3 (active thyroid hormone) can enter cardiomyocytes and alter gene expression, including upregulation of beta-adrenergic receptors, mimicking sympathetic stimulation. This increases HR and contractility. Excess leads to tachycardia.

questions.q: Which ion, when elevated in the blood, increases both heart rate and contractility?
opts: A. Potassium
B. Sodium
C. Calcium
D. Chloride
E. Magnesium
questions.exp: Elevated calcium (hypercalcaemia) increases HR and contractility by enhancing cardiomyocyte excitation-contraction coupling. It can shorten the QT interval and widen the T wave. Calcium channel blockers exploit this pathway to slow HR.

questions.q: A patient on a beta-blocker for hypertension has a lower resting heart rate. Which neurotransmitter's receptor is being blocked?
opts: A. Acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors
B. Norepinephrine at beta-1 adrenergic receptors
C. Epinephrine at alpha-1 receptors
D. Dopamine at D2 receptors
E. Serotonin at 5-HT3 receptors
questions.exp: Beta-blockers competitively block beta-1 adrenergic receptors, preventing norepinephrine and epinephrine from increasing HR and contractility. This is used in hypertension, heart failure, and arrhythmias.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly describes Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)?
opts: A. MAP = Systolic + Diastolic / 2
B. MAP = Diastolic + (Pulse Pressure / 3)
C. MAP = Systolic − Diastolic
D. MAP = HR × SV
E. MAP = CVP + CO
questions.exp: MAP = Diastolic + (Systolic − Diastolic) / 3. Because diastole occupies approximately two-thirds of the cardiac cycle at rest, diastolic pressure is weighted more heavily. Pulse pressure = Systolic − Diastolic.

questions.q: A patient has a BP of 120/80 mmHg. What is their pulse pressure?
opts: A. 200 mmHg
B. 93 mmHg
C. 40 mmHg
D. 80 mmHg
E. 60 mmHg
questions.exp: Pulse pressure = Systolic − Diastolic = 120 − 80 = 40 mmHg. MAP would be 80 + (40/3) ≈ 93 mmHg. A widened pulse pressure (>60) may indicate aortic regurgitation or increased arterial stiffness.

questions.q: Blood flow through vessels produces audible sounds during BP measurement. What are these sounds called?
opts: A. Heart murmurs
B. Korotkoff sounds
C. Bruit
D. Rales
E. Stridor
questions.exp: Korotkoff sounds are heard through a stethoscope during blood pressure measurement with a sphygmomanometer. They result from turbulent blood flow when the cuff partially occludes the artery. The first sound marks systolic pressure and disappearance marks diastolic.

questions.q: According to Poiseuille's law, which factor has the GREATEST effect on vascular resistance?
opts: A. Blood viscosity
B. Vessel length
C. Vessel radius
D. Blood velocity
E. Heart rate
questions.exp: Resistance = 8ηλ / πr⁴. Vessel radius is raised to the 4th power, making it by far the most powerful determinant of resistance. Halving the radius increases resistance 16-fold. This is why arterioles (resistance vessels) play the key role in regulating blood flow.

questions.q: A patient develops polycythaemia vera (increased RBCs). How does this primarily affect blood flow?
opts: A. Increases blood flow by raising oxygen delivery
B. Decreases resistance by increasing vessel diameter
C. Increases viscosity, raising resistance and decreasing blood flow
D. Increases compliance of arteries
E. Stimulates vasodilation via baroreceptors
questions.exp: Blood viscosity is determined largely by formed elements (especially RBCs) and plasma proteins. Polycythaemia raises RBC count, increasing viscosity, which increases resistance (Resistance ∝ viscosity) and therefore decreases blood flow at a given pressure.

questions.q: Which of the following will INCREASE blood flow through a vessel?
opts: A. Increased vessel length
B. Increased blood viscosity
C. Vasoconstriction
D. Vasodilation
E. Increased afterload
questions.exp: Vasodilation increases vessel radius, dramatically reducing resistance (R ∝ 1/r⁴), and therefore increasing blood flow. Vasoconstriction does the opposite. Increased length and viscosity both raise resistance and reduce flow.

questions.q: Veins contain approximately what percentage of total blood volume at any given time?
questions.exp: Systemic veins contain approximately 64% of total blood volume at rest, reflecting their high capacitance. This is why they are called capacitance vessels. They can distend readily to store large volumes at low pressure.

questions.q: Which two physiological pumps assist venous return to the heart?
opts: A. Cardiac pump and respiratory pump
B. Skeletal muscle pump and respiratory pump
C. Smooth muscle pump and lymphatic pump
D. Diaphragm pump and arterial recoil
E. Baroreceptor reflex and chemoreceptor reflex
questions.exp: Venous return is assisted by (1) the skeletal muscle pump — muscular contraction compresses veins and pushes blood toward the heart via one-way valves; and (2) the respiratory pump — inhalation decreases thoracic pressure, drawing blood into thoracic veins.

questions.q: Which layer of a blood vessel wall is most responsible for vasoconstriction and vasodilation?
opts: A. Tunica intima
B. Tunica media
C. Tunica externa
D. Endothelium
E. Basement membrane
questions.exp: The tunica media contains layers of smooth muscle and elastic fibres. Contraction of circular smooth muscle fibres (vasoconstriction) narrows the lumen, while relaxation (vasodilation) widens it. The tunica media is thickest in arteries.

questions.q: Which layer of a blood vessel contains the endothelium?
opts: A. Tunica externa
B. Tunica media
C. Tunica intima
D. External elastic membrane
E. Vasa vasorum
questions.exp: The tunica intima is the innermost layer, lined by a simple squamous epithelium called the endothelium. It is continuous throughout the entire vascular system and regulates exchange between blood and vessel walls, as well as altering blood flow.

questions.q: What is the function of the vasa vasorum?
opts: A. Control smooth muscle contraction in vessel walls
B. Provide nourishment to the cells of blood vessel walls
C. Regulate blood viscosity
D. Drain excess interstitial fluid
E. Detect blood pressure changes in vessel walls
questions.exp: Blood flows too fast through large vessels to nourish their own walls. The vasa vasorum are tiny blood vessels within the vessel walls (particularly the tunica externa and outer tunica media) that supply nutrients and oxygen to these cells. Arterial disease is partly attributed to difficulty nourishing arterial walls.

questions.q: The nervi vasorum refers to which structure?
opts: A. Baroreceptors within the aortic arch
B. Minute nerves within vessel walls controlling smooth muscle contraction
C. Sympathetic ganglia supplying the SA node
D. Branches of the vagus nerve to the coronary arteries
E. Lymphatic vessels accompanying blood vessels
questions.exp: The nervi vasorum are minute nerve fibres within the walls of blood vessels that control the contraction and dilation of smooth muscle in the tunica media, regulating vessel diameter and therefore blood flow and pressure.

questions.q: Which type of artery is characterised by a high proportion of elastic fibres, large lumen diameter, and the ability to expand and recoil with each heartbeat?
opts: A. Muscular artery
B. Arteriole
C. Elastic (conducting) artery
D. Metarteriole
E. Capillary
questions.exp: Elastic (conducting) arteries (e.g. aorta, pulmonary artery) have a high proportion of elastic fibres in all three tunics. They expand to accept the bolus of blood from ventricular systole and recoil during diastole, helping maintain a continuous pressure gradient.

questions.q: Muscular arteries differ from elastic arteries in that they:
opts: A. Are located closer to the heart
B. Have a thinner tunica media
C. Have a thick tunica media and play a greater role in vasoconstriction
D. Contain no smooth muscle
E. Are the primary site of gas exchange
questions.exp: Muscular arteries are located further from the heart and have a thick tunica media with a high proportion of smooth muscle. They are less distensible than elastic arteries and play a major role in vasoconstriction to regulate blood distribution.

questions.q: Arterioles are described as 'resistance vessels' because they:
opts: A. Have the largest lumens in the vascular system
B. Are critical in slowing blood flow due to their very small diameter
C. Contain the most valves
D. Have the highest proportion of elastic fibres
E. Are the primary site of nutrient exchange
questions.exp: Arterioles have very small diameters and only 1–2 layers of smooth muscle in the tunica media. Their small radius generates high resistance (R ∝ 1/r⁴), making them the primary regulators of blood flow distribution to capillary beds.

questions.q: Which type of capillary has the most permeable walls, with extensive intercellular gaps, incomplete basement membrane, and fenestrations, and is found in the liver and spleen?
opts: A. Continuous capillary
B. Fenestrated capillary
C. Sinusoid capillary
D. Metarteriole
E. Thoroughfare channel
questions.exp: Sinusoid (discontinuous) capillaries are the least common and most permeable, with large intercellular gaps, incomplete basement membrane, and fenestrations. They allow large molecules and cells to pass through and are found in the liver, spleen, bone marrow, and some endocrine glands.

questions.q: Fenestrated capillaries differ from continuous capillaries in that they:
opts: A. Have a complete endothelial lining with no gaps
B. Have pores through the endothelial lining allowing larger molecule exchange
C. Are found exclusively in the brain
D. Lack a basement membrane entirely
E. Cannot allow gas exchange
questions.exp: Fenestrated capillaries have pores (fenestrations) through the endothelial cells in addition to tight junctions. This makes them more permeable than continuous capillaries. They are found in the small intestine, kidneys, choroid plexus, and endocrine glands.

questions.q: Continuous capillaries in the brain differ from those elsewhere in the body because they:
opts: A. Lack a basement membrane
B. Are more permeable to large proteins
C. Form the blood-brain barrier with tight junctions and no intercellular clefts
D. Have large fenestrations to allow drug entry
E. Contain smooth muscle to regulate flow
questions.exp: Cerebral continuous capillaries form the blood-brain barrier. They have complete tight junctions with no intercellular clefts, a thick basal membrane, and astrocyte end-feet surrounding them. This strictly limits what can enter the CNS.

questions.q: Precapillary sphincters regulate blood flow by:
opts: A. Acting as valves in large veins to prevent backflow
B. Controlling flow from metarterioles into capillary beds based on local tissue needs
C. Increasing cardiac output during exercise
D. Detecting blood pressure changes and signalling the medulla
E. Generating the pulse wave felt at the wrist
questions.exp: Precapillary sphincters are rings of smooth muscle at the origin of capillaries from metarterioles. They open when local tissue O2 is low and metabolic waste products accumulate, allowing blood into that capillary bed. When closed, blood is shunted through thoroughfare channels.

questions.q: A patient with varicose veins has incompetent venous valves in their legs. Which of the following best explains why this causes venous pooling?
opts: A. Veins lose their tunica media and collapse
B. Blood cannot flow unidirectionally and pools due to gravity
C. Valves normally pump blood actively; without them there is no venous pressure
D. Venous smooth muscle atrophies without valve stimulation
E. Incompetent valves cause arteriovenous shunting
questions.exp: Venous valves ensure unidirectional blood flow toward the heart. When valves become incompetent, blood refluxes and pools due to gravitational forces, particularly in the lower limbs. The skeletal muscle pump cannot compensate adequately, leading to venous hypertension and varicosities.

questions.q: Hydrostatic pressure in capillaries (CHP) drives fluid in which direction?
opts: A. From interstitial fluid into capillaries (reabsorption)
B. From capillaries into interstitial fluid (filtration)
C. From lymphatic vessels into capillaries
D. From arterioles into venules
E. From plasma proteins into the interstitium
questions.exp: Capillary hydrostatic pressure (CHP) is the outward force of blood pressure against capillary walls. It drives filtration — fluid movement from the capillary lumen into the interstitial space. It is opposed by blood colloidal osmotic pressure (BCOP) and interstitial fluid hydrostatic pressure.

questions.q: Blood colloidal osmotic pressure (BCOP) is generated primarily by which component of blood?
opts: A. Erythrocytes
B. Electrolytes such as sodium and chloride
C. Plasma proteins (particularly albumin)
D. Glucose
E. Carbon dioxide
questions.exp: BCOP is generated by large plasma proteins (mainly albumin) that cannot cross the capillary membrane. These create an osmotic gradient that draws water back into the capillary (reabsorption), opposing filtration driven by CHP.

questions.q: At the arterial end of a capillary, net filtration pressure is approximately +10 mmHg. This means:
opts: A. Fluid is being reabsorbed into the capillary
B. There is no net fluid movement
C. Fluid is moving out of the capillary into the interstitium
D. Osmotic pressure exceeds hydrostatic pressure
E. The capillary is in venous territory
questions.exp: A positive net filtration pressure means CHP exceeds BCOP + IFHP, so fluid is pushed out of the capillary (filtration). At the arterial end CHP (~35 mmHg) exceeds BCOP (~26 mmHg), giving net outward movement. At the venous end CHP falls and reabsorption predominates.

questions.q: Which system is responsible for collecting the excess fluid filtered from capillaries that is not reabsorbed into the venous end?
opts: A. The portal venous system
B. The lymphatic system
C. The thoracic aorta
D. The pulmonary venous system
E. The sinusoids of the liver
questions.exp: Approximately 20 L of plasma is filtered into the interstitium per day; ~17 L is reabsorbed at the venous end of capillaries. The remaining ~3 L is collected by lymphatic capillaries and returned to the bloodstream via lymphatic ducts draining into the subclavian veins.

questions.q: A patient with severe hypoalbuminaemia (low albumin) develops oedema. Which physiological mechanism explains this?
opts: A. Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure pushes more fluid out
B. Increased arteriolar resistance reduces venous return
C. Reduced BCOP means less reabsorption occurs at venous end of capillaries
D. Lymphatic vessels become overactive and drain the interstitium
E. Venous valves become incompetent due to lack of protein
questions.exp: Albumin is the main contributor to BCOP. With low albumin, BCOP falls, reducing the osmotic force drawing fluid back into capillaries. Net filtration pressure increases throughout the capillary bed, and more fluid remains in the interstitium — causing oedema.

questions.q: Lymph is NOT actively pumped by the heart. Which mechanisms move lymph through lymphatic vessels?
opts: A. Cardiac contractions transmitted through arterial pulsations
B. Contraction of smooth muscle in lymphatic walls only
C. Skeletal muscle contractions and breathing movements
D. Pressure from the subclavian vein
E. Osmotic pressure from high protein lymph fluid
questions.exp: Lymph moves through skeletal muscle contractions (compressing lymphatic vessels) and breathing (respiratory pump changes intrathoracic pressure). One-way valves in lymphatic vessels ensure unidirectional flow. This is analogous to the venous pumps.

questions.q: Into which vein does the thoracic duct ultimately drain?
opts: A. Right subclavian vein
B. Right internal jugular vein
C. Left subclavian vein
D. Superior vena cava
E. Inferior vena cava
questions.exp: The thoracic duct drains into the left subclavian vein (at the junction with the left internal jugular vein). The right lymphatic duct drains into the right subclavian vein. The thoracic duct collects lymph from approximately three-quarters of the body.

questions.q: Which side of the body is drained by the right lymphatic duct?
opts: A. The entire lower body
B. The left upper limb and left thorax
C. The right upper limb, right side of the head, and right thorax
D. The right side of the abdomen and right lower limb
E. The entire right side of the body
questions.exp: The right lymphatic duct drains lymph from the right upper limb, the right side of the head and neck, and the right side of the thorax. All other regions — including the lower body and left upper limb — drain into the thoracic duct.

questions.q: In the small intestine, specialised lymphatic capillaries called lacteals absorb which substances?
opts: A. Glucose and amino acids
B. Water-soluble vitamins B and C
C. Dietary lipids and lipid-soluble vitamins
D. Iron and calcium
E. Electrolytes and water
questions.exp: Lacteals are lymphatic capillaries in the villi of the small intestine. They absorb dietary triglycerides, long-chain fatty acids, and lipid-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) packaged as chylomicrons. The resulting milky fluid is called chyle.

questions.q: A patient presents with pitting oedema of the legs following cancer surgery that involved lymph node removal. The most likely cause is:
opts: A. Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure from heart failure
B. Lymphatic obstruction preventing drainage of interstitial fluid
C. Hypoalbuminaemia from nutritional deficiency
D. Venous valve incompetence from thrombosis
E. Increased blood viscosity from polycythaemia
questions.exp: Removal of lymph nodes (lymphadenectomy) during cancer surgery can obstruct lymphatic drainage, causing lymphoedema. Excess interstitial fluid that cannot be returned to circulation accumulates in tissues, causing non-pitting or pitting oedema depending on severity.

questions.q: Which of the following is a feature of veins compared to arteries?
opts: A. Thicker walls and smaller lumens
B. Higher blood pressure
C. Thinner walls, larger lumens, and presence of valves
D. Greater proportion of smooth muscle in the tunica media
E. Absence of a tunica intima
questions.exp: Veins have thinner walls and larger, more irregular lumens than arteries. Many veins contain one-way valves to prevent backflow, especially in the lower limbs. Their walls have less smooth muscle and elastic tissue, as they operate under lower pressure.

questions.q: A patient's pulmonary artery is carrying blood from the right ventricle. What is the oxygen saturation of this blood?
opts: A. ~98% — fully oxygenated
B. ~75% — deoxygenated
C. ~50% — severely hypoxic
D. ~100% — pulmonary circulation carries oxygenated blood
E. Variable — depends on tidal volume
questions.exp: The pulmonary artery is unique: it carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs. Normal mixed venous oxygen saturation is ~75%. The pulmonary veins, conversely, carry oxygenated blood back to the left atrium.

questions.q: The pulmonary vasculature is described as a 'low pressure, high capacitance' system. What does this mean?
opts: A. Blood flows slowly and the vessels have thick, muscular walls
B. High pressures are needed to perfuse the lung bases
C. The system operates at low pressures and vessels distend easily to accommodate blood
D. High resistance prevents over-perfusion of alveoli
E. Pulmonary veins have the highest pressure in the circuit
questions.exp: The pulmonary circulation operates at much lower pressures than the systemic circulation (~25/10 mmHg vs 120/80 mmHg). Its vessels are thin-walled and highly distensible (high capacitance), optimised for gas exchange rather than bulk flow against resistance.

questions.q: A 55-year-old male smokes 30 cigarettes per day and is found to have stiffened, non-compliant arteries. What is the expected effect on blood pressure?
opts: A. Decreased blood pressure due to reduced cardiac output
B. Increased blood pressure due to reduced vascular compliance
C. No change as compliance only affects veins
D. Decreased blood pressure as stiff vessels cause vasodilation
E. Increased diastolic pressure only, with normal systolic pressure
questions.exp: Vascular compliance is the ability of a vessel to expand and contract. Stiff arteries (e.g. from atherosclerosis or smoking) have reduced compliance, meaning they cannot accommodate the pulse wave by expanding. This increases vascular resistance and raises blood pressure, particularly systolic pressure.

questions.q: The tunica externa (tunica adventitia) is the THICKEST layer in which vessel type?
opts: A. Elastic arteries
B. Muscular arteries
C. Arterioles
D. Veins
E. Capillaries
questions.exp: The tunica externa is the thickest layer in veins, sometimes exceeding the tunica media. It consists of collagenous and elastic fibres plus some smooth muscle. In veins, the externa provides structural support and contains the vasa vasorum and nervi vasorum.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the internal elastic membrane (lamina)?
opts: A. A layer of smooth muscle separating the tunica media from tunica externa
B. A thick distinct layer of elastic fibres forming the boundary between tunica intima and tunica media, present in large arteries
C. The basement membrane underlying the endothelium
D. A fenestrated layer found only in sinusoid capillaries
E. Collagenous fibres in the tunica externa
questions.exp: The internal elastic membrane is a distinct layer of elastic fibres at the boundary between the tunica intima and tunica media in large arteries. It has small openings permitting material exchange between the two layers. It is absent in veins, which is one histological way to distinguish arteries from veins.

questions.q: Which of the following factors increases stroke volume via increased preload?
opts: A. Increased parasympathetic stimulation
B. Increased venous return stretching the ventricle (Frank-Starling mechanism)
C. Increased afterload from hypertension
D. Administration of a beta-blocker
E. Decreased heart rate
questions.exp: Preload is the degree of ventricular filling at end-diastole (EDV). Increased venous return stretches the ventricular walls, increasing sarcomere length and contractile force (Frank-Starling law), which increases SV. Increased contractility (e.g. from sympathetic stimulation) also increases SV via a different mechanism.

questions.q: A patient is given a drug that blocks the sympathetic nervous system to the heart. Which of the following changes would you expect?
opts: A. Increased HR and increased contractility
B. Decreased HR and decreased contractility
C. Increased HR with decreased SV
D. No change as parasympathetic tone dominates
E. Vasodilation of arterioles only
questions.exp: Sympathetic stimulation normally increases HR (chronotropy) and contractility (inotropy). Blocking sympathetic input removes this drive, resulting in decreased HR and decreased SV. This is the mechanism of beta-blockers used in hypertension and heart failure.

questions.q: A patient with severe haemorrhage loses 30% of blood volume. Which of the following is the expected baroreceptor response?
opts: A. Increased parasympathetic tone to slow HR and lower BP
B. Decreased baroreceptor firing, leading to increased sympathetic stimulation and increased HR
C. Activation of chemoreceptors only with no cardiovascular change
D. Vasodilation to reduce afterload and protect remaining perfusion
E. Atrial reflex causes decreased HR
questions.exp: Haemorrhage reduces blood volume and BP, decreasing stretch on baroreceptors, which reduces their firing rate. The cardiovascular centre interprets this as low pressure and increases sympathetic stimulation (tachycardia, increased contractility, vasoconstriction) to restore BP — the baroreceptor reflex.

questions.q: Vasomotion in capillary beds refers to:
opts: A. Active pumping of blood by capillary endothelium
B. Irregular pulsating flow regulated by chemical signals in response to local tissue conditions
C. Rhythmic contraction of venular smooth muscle
D. The pulse wave transmitted from the aorta
E. Flow reversal in capillaries during diastole
questions.exp: Vasomotion is the irregular, pulsating flow of blood through capillaries, regulated by local chemical signals such as O2 levels, CO2, and metabolic waste products. It ensures that capillary beds open and close dynamically based on local tissue metabolic needs.

questions.q: Which of the following correctly distinguishes diapedesis?
opts: A. The process by which red blood cells pass through fenestrated capillaries
B. The movement of white blood cells through vessel walls into tissues at venules
C. Filtration of plasma proteins at the arterial end of capillaries
D. Reabsorption of lymph at the thoracic duct
E. The passage of platelets through the endothelium during clot formation
questions.exp: Diapedesis (emigration) is the process by which white blood cells adhere to the endothelial lining of venules and squeeze between adjacent endothelial cells to enter the interstitial fluid, particularly during inflammation. Venules and capillaries are the primary sites.

questions.q: A patient has a resting systolic BP of 145 mmHg and diastolic of 92 mmHg. What is their approximate MAP?
opts: A. 109 mmHg
B. 118 mmHg
C. 92 mmHg
D. 145 mmHg
E. 53 mmHg
questions.exp: MAP = Diastolic + (Systolic − Diastolic) / 3 = 92 + (145 − 92) / 3 = 92 + 53/3 = 92 + 17.7 ≈ 109.7 mmHg. A MAP above 100 mmHg at rest is abnormal and reflects hypertension.

questions.q: Systemic capillaries account for approximately what percentage of total blood volume?
questions.exp: Systemic capillaries hold approximately 7% of total blood volume. The majority (64%) is held in systemic veins. Systemic arteries hold 13%, and the heart and pulmonary circulation account for the remainder.

questions.q: Which statement about the tunica media is FALSE?
opts: A. It is the middle layer of blood vessel walls
B. It is thicker in arteries than in veins
C. It contains smooth muscle and elastic fibres
D. It is the thickest layer in large veins
E. Contraction of its circular smooth muscle causes vasoconstriction
questions.exp: The tunica media is the thickest layer in arteries, not veins. In veins, the tunica externa is typically the thickest layer. The tunica media contains smooth muscle and elastic fibres and is responsible for vasoconstriction and vasodilation.

questions.q: A 45-year-old runner completes a marathon. During the race, which of the following cardiovascular adaptations would you expect?
opts: A. Decreased HR, increased SV, increased CO
B. Increased HR, increased SV initially, greatly increased CO
C. Decreased CO with compensatory vasodilation
D. Increased parasympathetic tone to manage increased metabolic demand
E. Decreased venous return due to skeletal muscle vasoconstriction
questions.exp: During exercise, sympathetic stimulation increases HR and contractility (increasing SV). CO rises dramatically — up to 4–5× resting in untrained individuals, and 7–8× in elite athletes. Venous return also increases due to the skeletal muscle and respiratory pumps, and vasodilation in exercising muscle increases blood flow.

questions.q: The cisterna chyli is associated with which part of the lymphatic system?
opts: A. The right lymphatic duct at the right subclavian vein
B. The beginning of the thoracic duct just below the diaphragm
C. The lymphatic capillaries in the small intestinal villi
D. Lymph nodes in the right axilla
E. The thoracic duct at the level of the thoracic inlet
questions.exp: The cisterna chyli is a sac-like structure at the origin of the thoracic duct, just below the diaphragm. It receives lymph from the lower abdomen, pelvis, and lower limbs via the left and right lumbar trunks and the intestinal trunk.

questions.q: A patient develops right heart failure with elevated venous pressure. How would this affect capillary fluid dynamics?
opts: A. Increased BCOP causing increased reabsorption and reduced oedema
B. Reduced CHP causing fluid to remain in capillaries
C. Increased venous CHP causing increased filtration and oedema
D. Increased lymphatic drainage compensating for elevated pressure
E. Arteriolar vasoconstriction reducing capillary perfusion
questions.exp: Right heart failure causes elevated venous pressure, which backs up into the capillary bed, raising CHP. This increases net filtration pressure (more fluid leaves capillaries), overwhelming lymphatic drainage capacity and causing oedema — particularly in dependent areas like the ankles.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the function of the endothelium in the tunica intima?
opts: A. Provides structural support through collagen production only
B. Acts as a boundary between blood and vessel wall, regulates exchange and blood flow
C. Controls vasoconstriction via smooth muscle
D. Produces the pulse wave through rhythmic contraction
E. Contains valves to prevent backflow
questions.exp: The endothelium is a specialised simple squamous epithelium lining all blood vessels continuously. It forms the boundary between blood and vessel wall, regulates capillary exchange, produces vasoactive substances (e.g. nitric oxide for vasodilation), and modulates blood flow and clotting.

questions.q: During inspiration, intrathoracic pressure decreases. How does this contribute to venous return?
opts: A. Arterial blood pressure increases, pushing blood into the venae cavae
B. Pressure in thoracic veins falls below abdominal venous pressure, drawing blood toward the heart
C. Diaphragm compression of the abdomen reduces abdominal venous pressure
D. Increased intrathoracic pressure compresses thoracic veins
E. The lungs directly pump blood into the left atrium
questions.exp: During inhalation, the diaphragm descends and thoracic volume increases, causing intrathoracic pressure to fall. This lowers pressure in thoracic veins below that in abdominal veins, creating a pressure gradient that drives venous blood toward the right atrium — the respiratory pump.

questions.q: Which chemoreceptor-mediated signals increase HR and force of cardiac contraction?
opts: A. Increased O2 and decreased CO2
B. Decreased O2, increased CO2, increased H+, and increased lactic acid
C. Decreased lactic acid and increased pH
D. Increased O2 with decreased H+
E. Decreased CO2 and normal O2
questions.exp: Chemoreceptors detect metabolic changes indicating increased tissue activity or poor perfusion. Low O2, high CO2, elevated H+ (acidosis), and high lactic acid signal the cardiovascular centre to increase sympathetic drive, raising HR and contractility to improve oxygen delivery.

questions.q: What is the primary role of the lymph nodes through which lymph passes?
opts: A. Production of erythrocytes
B. Filtration of lymph and immune surveillance
C. Reabsorption of filtered plasma proteins
D. Secretion of clotting factors
E. Regulation of lymph flow rate
questions.exp: Lymph nodes filter lymph fluid, removing pathogens, debris, and cancer cells. They house lymphocytes (B and T cells) and macrophages for immune surveillance. There are approximately 500–600 lymph nodes in the human body, concentrated in the groin, axillae, neck, chest, and abdomen.

questions.q: A patient with atherosclerosis has significant narrowing (stenosis) of a coronary artery. Using Poiseuille's law, if the radius is halved, resistance increases by what factor?
opts: A. 2-fold
B. 4-fold
C. 8-fold
D. 16-fold
E. 32-fold
questions.exp: According to Poiseuille's law, resistance ∝ 1/r⁴. If radius is halved (r → r/2), resistance = 1/(r/2)⁴ = 16/r⁴ — a 16-fold increase. This dramatically reduces blood flow to the myocardium, explaining why even partial coronary stenosis causes significant ischaemia.

questions.q: The Starling forces at a capillary are: CHP = 35 mmHg, BCOP = 26 mmHg, IFHP ≈ 0 mmHg. What is the net filtration pressure?
opts: A. −9 mmHg (reabsorption)
B. 0 mmHg (equilibrium)
C. +9 mmHg (filtration)
D. +35 mmHg (filtration)
E. +61 mmHg (filtration)
questions.exp: Net filtration pressure = (CHP + interstitial colloid osmotic pressure) − (BCOP + IFHP). Simplified: NFP = CHP − BCOP − IFHP = 35 − 26 − 0 = +9 mmHg. A positive value means net filtration (fluid leaves the capillary). This occurs at the arterial end.

questions.q: Which of the following best explains why arterial disease is more common than venous disease?
opts: A. Arteries are exposed to higher osmotic pressures
B. Arterial blood contains more metabolic waste products
C. Vasa vasorum in arteries are under high pressure and situated in outer layers, making it harder to nourish arterial walls
D. Venous valves protect veins from high-pressure damage
E. Arteries contain more smooth muscle which predisposes them to inflammation
questions.exp: Arteries operate under high intraluminal pressure, meaning vasa vasorum must be located in the outer layers to avoid being collapsed. This makes it harder to nourish the inner arterial wall. Veins, with lower pressure, can have vasa vasorum closer to the lumen. This nutritional disadvantage is thought to contribute to the higher prevalence of arterial disease.

questions.q: A patient's cardiac output is measured at 6.0 L/min with a heart rate of 80 bpm. What is their stroke volume?
opts: A. 480 mL
B. 75 mL
C. 60 mL
E. Both B and D
questions.exp: SV = CO / HR = 6000 mL / 80 = 75 mL = 0.075 L. Both B (75 mL) and D (0.075 L) are the same value expressed in different units, so E is correct. This is within the normal range of 55–100 mL.

questions.q: A patient is given atropine (a muscarinic ACh receptor blocker). What would you expect to happen to their heart rate?
opts: A. HR decreases due to loss of parasympathetic tone
B. HR increases as parasympathetic inhibition is removed, allowing SA node to approach its intrinsic rate
C. HR is unchanged as atropine only affects smooth muscle
D. HR decreases then increases due to baroreceptor compensation
E. HR increases then decreases as sympathetic tone compensates
questions.exp: Atropine blocks muscarinic receptors, blocking parasympathetic (vagal) effects on the heart. This removes the brake on the SA node, allowing HR to approach its intrinsic rate of ~100 bpm or higher. Atropine is used clinically for symptomatic bradycardia.

questions.q: The thorax is bounded by which of the following structures?
opts: A. Vertebrae, ribs, costal cartilages, and the sternum
B. Vertebrae, clavicles, scapulae, and the sternum
C. Ribs, costal cartilages, diaphragm, and clavicles
D. Sternum, vertebrae, diaphragm, and clavicles
E. Ribs, vertebrae, manubrium, and xiphoid only
questions.exp: The thorax (chest) is bound by the vertebrae posteriorly, ribs laterally, costal cartilages, and the sternum anteriorly. The clavicles and scapulae are shoulder girdle bones, not thoracic cage boundaries.

questions.q: Which of the following structures does NOT reside in the mediastinum?
opts: A. Heart
B. Trachea
C. Oesophagus
D. Lung parenchyma
E. Great blood vessels
questions.exp: The mediastinum is the central compartment between the pleural sacs. It contains the heart, great blood vessels, trachea, oesophagus, and lymph nodes. Lung parenchyma lies within the pleural sacs, not in the mediastinum itself.

questions.q: At which vertebral level does the sternal angle (Angle of Louis) lie?
opts: A. T2
D. T8
E. T9
questions.exp: The sternal angle (Angle of Louis) lies at T4/5. It marks the manubriosternal junction and is a key landmark dividing the superior from the inferior mediastinum. T2 corresponds to the superior angle of the scapula; T8 is the inferior angle of the scapula; T9 is the xiphisternal joint.

questions.q: Which vertebral level corresponds to the xiphisternal joint?
opts: B. T7
C. T8
D. T9
E. L1
questions.exp: The xiphisternal joint lies at T9. T4/5 is the sternal angle; T8 is the inferior angle of the scapula; L3 corresponds to the lowest part of the costal margin.

questions.q: Costal cartilages connect ribs 8, 9, and 10 to which structure?
opts: A. The sternum directly
B. The cartilage of the rib above
C. The abdominal musculature
D. The xiphoid process
E. The thoracic vertebrae
questions.exp: Ribs 8, 9, and 10 are 'false ribs' — their costal cartilages connect to the cartilage of the rib above, not directly to the sternum. Ribs 1–7 connect directly to the sternum; ribs 11–12 are floating ribs that end in abdominal musculature.

questions.q: Which part of the sternum is composed of 4 'sternebrae' that fuse between puberty and age 25?
opts: A. Manubrium
B. Xiphoid process
C. Body of the sternum
D. Clavicular notch
E. Costal margin
questions.exp: The body of the sternum is composed of 4 sternebrae that fuse between puberty and around 25 years of age. The manubrium articulates with the clavicles and first costal cartilages. The xiphoid is the smallest and most inferior part.

questions.q: Which of the following accurately describes the xiphoid process?
opts: A. The largest part of the sternum
B. Composed of 4 fused sternebrae
C. Articulates with the clavicles
D. Often remains cartilaginous into adult life
E. Located at the level of T4/5
questions.exp: The xiphoid is the smallest part of the sternum and often remains cartilaginous into adult life, though it may later ossify. It lies at T9. It does not articulate with the clavicles — that is the manubrium.

questions.q: During inspiration, which of the following occurs?
opts: A. Respiratory muscles relax
B. Intrapleural pressure becomes positive
C. The diameters of the thorax increase and lung tissue expands
D. Elastic recoil of the lungs forces air out
E. The thoracic capacity reduces
questions.exp: During inspiration, the respiratory muscles contract, increasing the diameters of the thorax. This creates a negative intrapleural pressure, expanding the lung tissue and drawing air in. Elastic recoil and muscle relaxation describe expiration.

questions.q: The pericardium serves which of the following functions?
opts: A. Generates cardiac action potentials
B. Restricts heart movement and prevents overfilling with blood
C. Conducts impulses from the SA node to the AV node
D. Secretes hormones that regulate heart rate
E. Forms the myocardial layer of the heart wall
questions.exp: The pericardium is a sac that covers the heart. It restricts heart movements within the thoracic cavity and prevents overfilling with blood. The pericardial cavity contains serous fluid that lubricates and reduces friction during contraction.

questions.q: The pericardial cavity lies between which two layers?
opts: A. Fibrous pericardium and myocardium
B. Epicardium and myocardium
C. Parietal and visceral layers of the serous pericardium
D. Endocardium and myocardium
E. Fibrous pericardium and parietal pleura
questions.exp: The pericardial cavity is the thin space between the parietal layer (lining the inner surface of the fibrous pericardium) and the visceral layer (covering the outside of the heart) of the serous pericardium. This space contains serous fluid that reduces friction.

questions.q: Which layer of the heart wall is the thickest and is composed of cardiac muscle tissue?
opts: A. Epicardium
B. Endocardium
C. Pericardium
D. Myocardium
E. Fibrous pericardium
questions.exp: The myocardium is the middle and thickest layer of the heart wall, composed of cardiac muscle tissue. The epicardium is the outer layer; the endocardium is the inner layer lining the chambers.

questions.q: The endocardium is continuous with which structure in blood vessels?
opts: A. Tunica media
B. Tunica adventitia
C. Endothelium
D. Pericardium
E. Smooth muscle
questions.exp: The endocardium is the innermost layer of the heart, composed of simple squamous epithelium. It covers the valves and is continuous with the endothelium that lines the interior of blood vessels.

questions.q: Approximately what proportion of the heart lies to the left of the midline?
questions.exp: Approximately 2/3 of the heart lies to the left of the midline, with 1/3 to the right. It sits in the mediastinum between the lungs, posterior to the sternum and superior to the diaphragm.

questions.q: The right ventricle pumps blood into which vessel?
opts: A. Aorta
B. Superior vena cava
C. Pulmonary trunk
D. Coronary sinus
E. Inferior vena cava
questions.exp: The right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood into the pulmonary trunk, which bifurcates into the left and right pulmonary arteries leading to the lungs for gas exchange. The aorta receives blood from the left ventricle.

questions.q: After gas exchange in the pulmonary capillaries, oxygenated blood returns to the heart via which vessels?
opts: A. Pulmonary arteries
B. Superior and inferior vena cava
C. Coronary veins
D. Pulmonary veins
E. Bronchial arteries
questions.exp: Oxygenated blood returns from the lungs to the left atrium via the pulmonary veins (four in total). The pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood to the lungs. The vena cavae return systemic deoxygenated blood to the right atrium.

questions.q: The fossa ovalis is found in which part of the heart?
opts: A. Interventricular septum
B. Right ventricle
C. Interatrial septum
D. Left atrium
E. Atrioventricular septum
questions.exp: The fossa ovalis is an oval-shaped depression in the interatrial septum. It is the remnant of the foramen ovale, an opening in the fetal heart that allowed blood to bypass the pulmonary circuit. It closes after birth when the septum primum acts as a valve.

questions.q: Why is the interventricular septum thicker than the interatrial septum?
opts: A. It contains more adipose tissue
B. The ventricles generate greater pressure during contraction than the atria
C. It must support the weight of both atria
D. It is composed of fibrous rather than muscular tissue
E. It contains the His-Purkinje conduction system
questions.exp: The interventricular septum is thicker because ventricles generate significantly more pressure during contraction than atria, requiring stronger walls. It is a physical extension of the myocardium lined with endocardium.

questions.q: Which of the following structures drains the heart's myocardium and empties into the right atrium?
opts: A. Superior vena cava
B. Inferior vena cava
C. Coronary sinus
D. Great cardiac vein
E. Pulmonary trunk
questions.exp: The coronary sinus is a thin-walled vessel on the posterior surface of the heart that drains most of the coronary veins (great, middle, small, and posterior cardiac veins) and empties into the right atrium. The vena cavae drain systemic circulation.

questions.q: The right atrium receives blood from which three sources?
opts: A. Pulmonary veins, aorta, and coronary sinus
B. Superior vena cava, inferior vena cava, and coronary sinus
C. Pulmonary arteries, coronary veins, and superior vena cava
D. Inferior vena cava, pulmonary trunk, and coronary sinus
E. Superior vena cava, pulmonary veins, and inferior vena cava
questions.exp: The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from three sources: the superior vena cava (from superior to the diaphragm), the inferior vena cava (inferior to the diaphragm), and the coronary sinus (draining the myocardium itself).

questions.q: Pectinate muscles are found in which cardiac chambers?
opts: A. Both ventricles
B. Left atrium and left ventricle
C. Right atrium and right auricle, and the left auricle
D. All four chambers equally
E. Right ventricle only
questions.exp: Pectinate muscles are prominent muscle ridges found in the right atrium, the right auricle, and the left auricle. The left atrium itself does not have pectinate muscles (except in its auricle), giving it a mostly smooth interior.

questions.q: The chordae tendineae connect the AV valve leaflets to which structure?
opts: A. Trabeculae carneae
B. Papillary muscles
C. Moderator band
D. Fibrous cardiac skeleton
E. Interventricular septum
questions.exp: The chordae tendineae ('heart strings') are strong connective tissue cords composed of ~80% collagen and 20% elastin that connect the AV valve flaps (leaflets) to the papillary muscles. This arrangement prevents valve prolapse during ventricular contraction.

questions.q: What is the primary function of the papillary muscles contracting during ventricular systole?
opts: A. To open the semilunar valves
B. To increase ventricular volume
C. To prevent backflow through the AV valves by tensioning the chordae tendineae
D. To conduct electrical impulses to the Purkinje fibres
E. To eject blood into the pulmonary trunk
questions.exp: During ventricular contraction, the papillary muscles contract simultaneously with the ventricular walls. This puts tension on the chordae tendineae, preventing the AV valve leaflets from prolapsing (inverting) back into the atria, thus preventing regurgitation.

questions.q: The moderator band in the right ventricle serves which function?
opts: A. Prevents overfilling of the right ventricle
B. Reinforces the ventricular wall and carries part of the cardiac conduction system
C. Connects the tricuspid valve to the aortic valve
D. Separates the right ventricle from the left ventricle
E. Drains venous blood from the right ventricle
questions.exp: The moderator band is a band of cardiac muscle covered by endocardium that crosses the interior of the right ventricle from the interventricular septum to the anterior papillary muscle. It reinforces the ventricular walls and carries part of the cardiac conduction system.

questions.q: How many cusps does the mitral (bicuspid) valve have?
questions.exp: The mitral (bicuspid) valve has 2 cusps, distinguishing it from the tricuspid valve on the right side which has 3 leaflets. Both have chordae tendineae and papillary muscles. The mitral valve sits between the left atrium and left ventricle.

questions.q: Which valves lack papillary muscles and chordae tendineae?
opts: A. Tricuspid and mitral valves
B. Mitral and aortic valves
C. Pulmonary and aortic semilunar valves
D. Tricuspid and pulmonary valves
E. All four cardiac valves have papillary muscles
questions.exp: The semilunar valves (pulmonary and aortic) do not have papillary muscles or chordae tendineae. They rely on the pressure of blood filling their cusps to close. The AV valves (tricuspid and mitral) require chordae tendineae and papillary muscles to prevent prolapse.

questions.q: The aortic semilunar valve closes when which pressure relationship occurs?
opts: A. Left ventricular pressure exceeds aortic pressure
B. Aortic pressure exceeds left ventricular pressure at the end of systole
C. Right ventricular pressure falls below pulmonary pressure
D. Atrial pressure exceeds ventricular pressure
E. Ventricular pressure equals zero
questions.exp: The aortic valve closes when aortic pressure exceeds left ventricular pressure at the end of systole. Blood attempts to flow back toward the ventricle, filling the valve cusps and closing them, producing the S2 ('dupp') heart sound.

questions.q: Coronary arteries arise from which part of the aorta?
opts: A. The aortic arch
B. The descending thoracic aorta
C. Just superior to the aortic semilunar valve (the aortic sinuses)
D. The abdominal aorta
E. The ascending aorta at the level of T4
questions.exp: The two coronary arteries arise from the beginning of the aorta, specifically from dilations called the aortic sinuses located just superior to the aortic semilunar valve. There are three aortic sinuses; the right posterior sinus gives rise to no vessel.

questions.q: The left anterior descending (LAD) artery follows which sulcus?
opts: A. Coronary sulcus
B. Posterior interventricular sulcus
C. Anterior interventricular sulcus
D. Atrioventricular sulcus
E. Oblique sinus
questions.exp: The LAD (also called the anterior interventricular artery) follows the anterior interventricular sulcus around the pulmonary trunk. It supplies the anterior interventricular septum and gives rise to branches that form anastomoses with the posterior interventricular artery.

questions.q: Which coronary vessel supplies the right atrium, portions of both ventricles, and the cardiac conduction system?
opts: A. Left coronary artery
B. Circumflex artery
C. Anterior interventricular artery
D. Right coronary artery
E. Great cardiac vein
questions.exp: The right coronary artery proceeds along the coronary sulcus and supplies the right atrium, portions of both ventricles, and the heart's conduction system (including the SA and AV nodes in most individuals).

questions.q: The anterior cardiac veins are unique among cardiac veins because they:
opts: A. Drain into the coronary sinus like all other cardiac veins
B. Drain directly into the right atrium, bypassing the coronary sinus
C. Drain into the left atrium
D. Run parallel to the circumflex artery
E. Drain exclusively into the great cardiac vein
questions.exp: The anterior cardiac veins are unique in that they drain the anterior surface of the right ventricle and empty directly into the right atrium, bypassing the coronary sinus. All other major cardiac veins (great, middle, small, posterior) drain into the coronary sinus first.

questions.q: The SA node is located at which anatomical junction?
opts: A. Junction of the inferior vena cava and right atrium
B. Junction of the superior vena cava and right atrium
C. Between the right and left atria
D. At the base of the interventricular septum
E. Within the AV node
questions.exp: The sinoatrial (SA) node is located at the junction of the superior vena cava and the right atrium. It is the primary pacemaker of the heart and initiates the electrical impulse that spreads across both atria.

questions.q: What is the primary role of the AV node in cardiac conduction?
opts: A. To initiate the cardiac impulse
B. To rapidly distribute the impulse to the ventricular myocardium
C. To delay impulse transmission to allow atrial contraction and ventricular filling
D. To repolarize the atria
E. To prevent impulses travelling from ventricles to atria only
questions.exp: The AV node deliberately delays impulse transmission (contributing 0.09s of the total 0.16s AV delay). This ensures the atria have time to contract and fill the ventricles before ventricular contraction begins, optimising cardiac output.

questions.q: The total AV delay from SA node to ventricular activation is approximately:
opts: A. 0.03 seconds
B. 0.09 seconds
C. 0.16 seconds
D. 0.4 seconds
E. 1.0 seconds
questions.exp: The total AV delay is 0.16 seconds, composed of: SA node to AV node (0.03s), AV node slow conduction (0.09s), and AV bundle/Bundle of His (0.04s). This ensures atrial contraction precedes ventricular contraction.

questions.q: Which component of the conducting system rapidly distributes the impulse throughout the ventricular myocardium?
opts: A. SA node
B. AV node
C. Bundle of His
D. Purkinje fibre network
E. Internodal pathways
questions.exp: The Purkinje fibre network rapidly distributes the impulse throughout the ventricular myocardium, ensuring coordinated ventricular contraction. The Bundle of His penetrates the membranous septum; the bundle branches split it left and right before the Purkinje fibres.

questions.q: The intrinsic firing rate of the SA node without autonomic modulation is approximately:
opts: A. 40–60 bpm
B. 60–80 bpm
E. 15–40 bpm
questions.exp: The intrinsic firing rate of the SA node is 100–110 bpm. In practice, constant vagal (parasympathetic) suppression reduces this to approximately 70 bpm at rest. If the SA node fails, the AV node takes over at 40–60 bpm.

questions.q: What are 'funny channels' (If channels) in pacemaker cells?
opts: A. Ligand-gated channels that open in response to acetylcholine
B. Hyperpolarization-activated channels that slowly allow Na+/K+ influx, gradually depolarizing the cell
C. Voltage-gated K+ channels responsible for repolarization
D. L-type Ca2+ channels that drive the action potential upstroke
E. Gap junctions between adjacent pacemaker cells
questions.exp: Funny channels (If) are hyperpolarization-activated, voltage-gated channels that open when the membrane becomes more negative. They slowly leak Na+ (and some K+) inward, gradually depolarizing the membrane toward the threshold of -40mV. Ca2+ channels then drive the upstroke.

questions.q: At what membrane potential does the SA node action potential upstroke begin?
opts: A. -90 mV
B. -70 mV
C. -60 mV (RMP) rising to -40 mV threshold
D. 0 mV
E. +30 mV
questions.exp: The SA node resting membrane potential (RMP) is approximately -60mV. The funny channels gradually depolarize the cell until the threshold of -40mV is reached, at which point L-type Ca2+ channels open and drive the upstroke of the action potential.

questions.q: Cardiac muscle cells are electrically coupled by which structures that allow direct ion flow between cells?
opts: A. T-tubules
B. Desmosomes
C. Gap junctions within intercalated discs
D. Sarcoplasmic reticulum
E. Tight junctions
questions.exp: Gap junctions within intercalated discs act as low-resistance electrical bridges, allowing action potentials to propagate directly between cardiac muscle cells without a synapse. This makes cardiac muscle function as a syncytium, ensuring coordinated contraction.

questions.q: What is the role of T-tubules in cardiac muscle cells?
opts: A. To store Ca2+ between contractions
B. To resist mechanical pulling forces during contraction
C. To ensure action potentials reach central myofibrils and couple electrical signals to Ca2+ release
D. To remove Ca2+ from the cytosol after contraction
E. To allow ion flow between adjacent cells
questions.exp: T-tubules are deep membrane invaginations that ensure action potentials reach the central myofibrils. They couple electrical signals to Ca2+ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) and are more prominent in cardiac muscle than in skeletal muscle.

questions.q: In excitation-contraction coupling in cardiac muscle, what triggers Ca2+-induced Ca2+ release (CICR)?
opts: A. K+ influx through funny channels
B. A small Ca2+ influx via L-type channels in the T-tubule membrane
C. Na+ influx through fast channels
D. Acetylcholine binding to muscarinic receptors
E. ATP hydrolysis by SERCA
questions.exp: A small Ca2+ influx through L-type (voltage-gated) Ca2+ channels in the T-tubule membrane triggers a much larger Ca2+ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum — this is CICR. The SR provides approximately 70% of the activation Ca2+, while the ECF provides ~30%.
