questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a mechanism for removing Ca2+ from the cytosol during cardiac muscle relaxation?
opts: A. SERCA (Ca2+-ATPase) pumping Ca2+ back into the SR
B. Na+/Ca2+ exchanger (NCX) removing Ca2+ across the sarcolemma
C. Closure of L-type Ca2+ channels at the end of the plateau phase
D. Ryanodine receptor opening on the SR
E. Reduction of cytosolic Ca2+ below troponin C binding threshold
questions.exp: Ryanodine receptors open during CICR to release Ca2+ from the SR — this is a contraction mechanism, not relaxation. Relaxation requires: L-type channel closure, SERCA pumping Ca2+ back into SR, and the NCX (3 Na+ in, 1 Ca2+ out) removing Ca2+ to the ECF.

questions.q: The Na+/Ca2+ exchanger (NCX) is described as 'electrogenic' because:
opts: A. It uses ATP directly
B. It exchanges equal numbers of Na+ and Ca2+ ions
C. It exchanges 3 Na+ in for 1 Ca2+ out, generating a net electric potential
D. It only operates during depolarization
E. It generates ATP as a by-product
questions.exp: The NCX is electrogenic because it exchanges 3 Na+ ions inward for 1 Ca2+ outward. This unequal charge exchange generates a net inward positive current, contributing to the membrane potential. It is a secondary active transporter driven by the Na+ gradient.

questions.q: Atrial fibrillation (AF) is caused by the loss of which structure's function?
opts: A. AV node
B. Purkinje fibres
C. SA node (SAN)
D. Bundle of His
E. Left bundle branch
questions.exp: Loss of SA node function results in atrial fibrillation, where atrial contraction becomes disorganised. Without coordinated atrial contraction, the 'atrial kick' (15–25% of ventricular filling) is lost, reducing cardiac efficiency. 80% of adults with AF are over 65.

questions.q: In the cardiac pacemaker hierarchy, if the SA node fails and the AV node becomes the pacemaker, what is the expected heart rate?
opts: B. 70–80 bpm
C. 40–60 bpm
D. 15–40 bpm
E. 120 bpm
questions.exp: The AV node is the secondary pacemaker with an intrinsic rate of 40–60 bpm. The Purkinje fibres are the tertiary pacemaker at 15–40 bpm, firing only in complete AV block to prevent cardiac arrest. The SA node's faster rate normally suppresses lower pacemakers.

questions.q: On an ECG, the P wave represents:
opts: A. Ventricular depolarization
B. Atrial depolarization
C. The delay at the AV node
D. Ventricular repolarization
E. Atrial repolarization
questions.exp: The P wave represents atrial depolarization, which initiates atrial contraction. The PR segment represents the delay at the AV node. The QRS complex represents ventricular depolarization. The T wave represents ventricular repolarization. Atrial repolarization is hidden within the QRS.

questions.q: The QRS complex on an ECG represents which electrical event?
opts: A. Atrial depolarization
B. AV node delay
C. Ventricular depolarization
D. Ventricular repolarization
E. Atrial repolarization
questions.exp: The QRS complex represents ventricular depolarization, triggering ventricular contraction. Atrial repolarization also occurs during this time but is obscured by the much larger QRS complex. The T wave represents ventricular repolarization.

questions.q: Which ECG lead is best for showing upright P waves and is commonly used to assess cardiac rhythm?
opts: A. Lead I
B. Lead II
C. Lead III
D. aVR
E. V1
questions.exp: Lead II best shows upright P waves because its positive electrode (left leg) is oriented toward the normal direction of atrial depolarization. It looks at the inferior wall of the heart and is widely used as the standard rhythm assessment lead.

questions.q: The augmented lead aVF has its positive electrode at the left leg and primarily views which wall of the heart?
opts: A. Lateral wall
B. Anterior wall
C. Posterior wall
D. Inferior wall
E. Septal wall
questions.exp: aVF has its positive electrode at the left leg and looks at the heart from an inferior perspective, viewing the inferior wall. It is useful for detecting inferior wall abnormalities such as inferior MI. Its reference is the mathematical average of the right arm (RA) and left arm (LA).

questions.q: When calculating the QRS axis, if Lead I is positive and aVF is negative, the axis is:
opts: A. Normal (0 to +90°)
B. Right axis deviation
C. Left axis deviation
D. Extreme axis deviation
E. Indeterminate
questions.exp: If Lead I is positive and aVF is negative, the QRS axis lies in the left quadrant (-30 to -90°), indicating left axis deviation (LAD). Causes include left-sided conduction delay and inferior MI. Both positive = normal; both negative = extreme deviation.

questions.q: Right axis deviation on ECG (+90° to +180°) is commonly associated with which condition?
opts: A. Left bundle branch block
B. Inferior myocardial infarction
C. Right heart strain or hypertrophy
D. Left ventricular hypertrophy
E. Atrial fibrillation
questions.exp: Right axis deviation (+90 to +180°) is commonly caused by right heart strain or hypertrophy, which shifts the dominant electrical force to the right. Left axis deviation is associated with left-sided conduction delay or infarction.

questions.q: The chest leads (V1–V6) on an ECG provide which view of the heart?
opts: A. Frontal plane view
B. Transverse (horizontal) plane view
C. Sagittal plane view
D. Coronal plane view only
E. An inferior view
questions.exp: The chest (precordial) leads V1–V6 look at a transverse view of the heart and are particularly useful for localising myocardial infarction by identifying ST-segment changes in specific territories.

questions.q: A 62-year-old man presents with central crushing chest pain radiating to his left arm, worse on exertion. What is the most likely underlying mechanism?
opts: A. Pericarditis causing friction rub
B. Coronary artery narrowing due to atherosclerosis causing a mismatch between myocardial oxygen supply and demand
C. Left ventricular hypertrophy reducing cardiac output
D. Pulmonary embolism obstructing the pulmonary circulation
E. Aortic dissection separating the aortic wall layers
questions.exp: This presentation describes ischaemic chest pain (angina). Atherosclerosis narrows the coronary arteries, reducing blood supply. During increased demand (exertion), there is a mismatch between oxygen supply and demand, causing the characteristic squeezing, heaviness, or tightness.

questions.q: Orthopnoea is best defined as:
opts: A. Breathlessness that wakes a patient from sleep
B. Breathlessness triggered only by exercise
C. Breathlessness that develops when a patient is supine, relieved by sitting up
D. Breathlessness due to pulmonary embolism
E. Breathlessness associated with right-sided heart failure only
questions.exp: Orthopnoea is breathlessness that develops when the patient lies flat (supine). When lying down, interstitial oedema redistributes to lung bases and venous return from the lower limbs increases, exacerbating demands on a failing left ventricle.

questions.q: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea (PND) is most characteristically due to:
opts: A. Right ventricular dysfunction
B. Left ventricular (LV) dysfunction causing episodes of breathlessness that wake the patient from sleep
C. Obstructive sleep apnoea
D. Pericardial effusion
E. Anxiety-related hyperventilation
questions.exp: PND occurs in left ventricular dysfunction. When the patient lies flat during sleep, fluid redistribution increases pulmonary venous pressure enough to cause breathlessness that wakes the patient. It is distinct from orthopnoea in that it occurs after the patient has been asleep.

questions.q: Ankle oedema in heart failure is most commonly associated with which type of failure?
opts: A. Left-sided heart failure
B. Congestive or right-sided heart failure
C. High-output heart failure
D. Diastolic dysfunction only
E. Systolic dysfunction of the left ventricle
questions.exp: Peripheral oedema (ankle oedema) is a sign of fluid overload and is associated with congestive or right-sided heart failure. Elevated right heart pressures cause systemic venous congestion, resulting in fluid accumulation in dependent areas.

questions.q: Intermediate claudication is caused by:
opts: A. Coronary artery disease causing calf pain at rest
B. Atherosclerosis in peripheral arteries supplying the legs, causing a mismatch of oxygen supply and demand on exertion
C. Deep vein thrombosis causing leg pain
D. Venous insufficiency causing bilateral leg oedema
E. Peripheral neuropathy causing bilateral calf cramping
questions.exp: Intermittent claudication is a cramping pain in the calf, thigh, or buttock on exertion, caused by atherosclerosis narrowing the peripheral arteries supplying the legs. The resulting mismatch between muscle oxygen supply and demand during exercise produces the characteristic symptoms.

questions.q: During the phase of isovolumetric contraction in the cardiac cycle:
opts: A. The aortic valve is open and blood is being ejected
B. All valves are closed, ventricular pressure is rising, and volume is constant
C. The mitral valve is open and blood is flowing from the left atrium
D. The ventricle is relaxing after ejection
E. The pulmonary valve opens and blood enters the lungs
questions.exp: Isovolumetric contraction follows mitral valve closure (end diastole). All valves are closed, so ventricular volume remains constant while pressure rises rapidly. This phase ends when ventricular pressure exceeds aortic pressure, causing the aortic valve to open.

questions.q: The 'atrial kick' contributes what percentage of ventricular filling?
questions.exp: The atrial kick (active atrial contraction at the end of diastole) contributes approximately 15–25% of total ventricular filling. Most ventricular filling (75–85%) occurs passively. Loss of this kick in AF significantly reduces cardiac efficiency, especially in diseased hearts.

questions.q: The dicrotic notch on an aortic pressure waveform represents:
opts: A. Opening of the aortic valve
B. Peak ventricular ejection pressure
C. A brief backward flow of blood toward the aortic valve at closure, causing a transient pressure drop followed by elastic recoil
D. Closure of the mitral valve
E. The end of isovolumetric relaxation
questions.exp: The dicrotic notch is caused by a tiny backward flow of blood toward the aortic valve as the ventricle relaxes. This closes the aortic valve and causes a brief drop in aortic pressure, followed by a rebound rise as elastic recoil of the aorta continues diastolic runoff.

questions.q: The end-systolic pressure-volume relationship (ESPVR) slope reflects which cardiac property?
opts: A. Ventricular compliance during filling
B. Myocardial contractility (how strongly the ventricle contracts)
C. Preload at end diastole
D. Afterload from peripheral resistance
E. Stroke volume per beat
questions.exp: The ESPVR slope is a load-independent measure of myocardial contractility — the steeper the slope, the stronger the ventricle contracts. The End-Diastolic Pressure-Volume Relationship (EDPVR) curve describes ventricular stiffness (compliance) during filling.

questions.q: During isovolumetric relaxation:
opts: A. The aortic valve is open and blood continues to flow into the aorta
B. The mitral valve opens immediately
C. All valves are closed and ventricular pressure falls rapidly after aortic valve closure
D. Atrial contraction provides additional ventricular filling
E. Ventricular volume increases as filling begins
questions.exp: Isovolumetric relaxation begins with aortic valve closure (end systole). All valves remain closed, so ventricular volume is constant. Ventricular pressure falls rapidly until it drops below left atrial pressure, at which point the mitral valve opens and rapid filling begins.

questions.q: The S1 heart sound ('lubb') is produced by:
opts: A. Opening of the aortic and pulmonary valves
B. Closure of the AV valves (tricuspid and mitral) at the start of systole
C. Closure of the semilunar valves at the end of systole
D. Turbulent flow through stenosed coronary arteries
E. Opening of the mitral valve at the start of diastole
questions.exp: S1 ('lubb') is produced by the closure of the AV valves (tricuspid and mitral) at the beginning of systole. S2 ('dupp') is produced by the closure of the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonary) at the end of systole.

questions.q: A patient with mitral stenosis would be expected to produce which auscultatory finding?
opts: A. A soft S2
B. A systolic murmur due to AV valve incompetence
C. A diastolic whistle murmur across the stenosed mitral valve
D. An absent S1
E. A pericardial friction rub
questions.exp: Stenosis of the mitral valve causes a murmur (typically a whistle murmur) as blood is forced through the narrowed valve during diastole. Incompetence of AV valves causes a gurgle murmur. Semilunar valve stenosis also produces whistle murmurs (systolic).

questions.q: A 45-year-old woman with iron deficiency anaemia presents with palpitations. Which mechanism best explains this?
opts: A. Excess potassium causing arrhythmias
B. Reduced haemoglobin causes oxygen deficiency, triggering compensatory tachycardia and heightened sympathetic activity
C. Anaemia directly damages the SA node
D. Low haemoglobin increases preload causing ventricular hypertrophy
E. Iron deficiency causes AV block
questions.exp: Iron deficiency reduces haemoglobin, decreasing the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity. The body compensates by increasing heart rate (tachycardia) via heightened sympathetic activity. This increased cardiac activity is perceived as palpitations.

questions.q: Syncope from severe aortic stenosis is best explained by:
opts: A. Increased venous return overwhelming the right heart
B. A drop in cardiac output below the threshold needed to maintain brain perfusion
C. Excessive vagal tone slowing the heart too much
D. Pulmonary oedema impairing oxygenation
E. Rapid AF causing haemorrhagic stroke
questions.exp: Aortic stenosis obstructs left ventricular outflow, limiting cardiac output. During exertion (when demand rises), the fixed obstruction prevents adequate output increase, causing a sudden drop in blood pressure below the threshold for cerebral perfusion, resulting in syncope.

questions.q: The circumflex artery originates from which vessel and follows which groove?
opts: A. Right coronary artery; posterior interventricular sulcus
B. Left coronary artery; coronary sulcus to the left
C. Aortic arch; anterior interventricular sulcus
D. Pulmonary trunk; atrioventricular sulcus
E. Left coronary artery; posterior interventricular sulcus
questions.exp: The circumflex artery arises from the left coronary artery and follows the coronary sulcus to the left, eventually fusing with small branches of the right coronary artery. It supplies the left atrium and lateral/posterior left ventricle.

questions.q: Which statement about the left ventricle compared to the right ventricle is correct?
opts: A. The left ventricle has a moderator band; the right does not
B. The left ventricle is thinner-walled to allow greater compliance
C. The left ventricle is thicker-walled to overcome greater resistance in the systemic circuit
D. Both ventricles have equal wall thickness
E. The right ventricle contains the mitral valve
questions.exp: The left ventricle has a thicker muscular wall than the right because it must generate sufficient pressure to overcome the high resistance of the long systemic circuit (versus the low resistance pulmonary circuit for the right ventricle). The right ventricle has the moderator band; the left does not.

questions.q: The great cardiac vein parallels which coronary artery?
opts: A. Right coronary artery
B. Posterior interventricular artery
C. Anterior interventricular (LAD) artery
D. Circumflex artery
E. Marginal artery
questions.exp: The great cardiac vein is first seen on the heart surface following the anterior interventricular sulcus, paralleling the LAD (anterior interventricular artery). It then flows along the coronary sulcus into the coronary sinus on the posterior surface.

questions.q: Which serous membrane directly covers the outer surface of the heart and forms part of the heart wall?
opts: A. Parietal pericardium
B. Fibrous pericardium
C. Visceral pericardium (epicardium)
D. Parietal pleura
E. Visceral pleura
questions.exp: The visceral pericardium, also called the epicardium, is fused directly to the outer surface of the heart and forms the outermost layer of the heart wall. The parietal pericardium lines the inner surface of the fibrous pericardium; together they form the serous pericardium.

questions.q: A medical student notes a patient's ECG shows Lead I is negative and aVF is positive. What does this indicate?
opts: A. Normal QRS axis
B. Left axis deviation
C. Right axis deviation
D. Extreme axis deviation
E. Ventricular tachycardia
questions.exp: When Lead I is negative and aVF is positive, the QRS axis falls in the right quadrant (+90 to +180°), indicating right axis deviation (RAD). Common causes include right heart strain and hypertrophy. Both negative would indicate extreme deviation.

questions.q: The foramen ovale in the fetal heart allows blood to:
opts: A. Bypass the coronary circulation
B. Flow from the right ventricle directly to the aorta
C. Pass directly from the right atrium to the left atrium, bypassing the pulmonary circuit
D. Flow from the left atrium to the right ventricle
E. Bypass the AV node for faster conduction
questions.exp: The foramen ovale in the fetal heart allows blood to pass directly from the right atrium to the left atrium, bypassing the non-functional pulmonary circuit in utero. After birth, the septum primum closes the foramen ovale, leaving the fossa ovalis as a remnant.

questions.q: The 'cardiac skeleton' consists of dense connective tissue rings around which structures?
opts: A. Coronary artery ostia
B. The four valve openings (mitral, tricuspid, aortic, pulmonary)
C. The vena cava orifices
D. The pulmonary veins
E. The SA and AV nodes
questions.exp: The cardiac skeleton consists of four fibrous rings of dense connective tissue surrounding the four valve openings. It provides structural support, maintains valve geometry, and forms an important electrical boundary — it electrically isolates the atria from the ventricles so conduction must pass only through the AV node.

questions.q: When is the AV (mitral) valve open?
opts: A. When ventricular pressure exceeds atrial pressure (systole)
B. When ventricular pressure is less than atrial pressure (diastole — passive filling)
C. Only during isovolumetric contraction
D. Only when the atria contract
E. When semilunar valves are also open
questions.exp: The AV valves (including the mitral) open when ventricular pressure falls below atrial pressure during diastole, allowing passive ventricular filling. They close at the onset of systole when ventricular pressure rises to exceed atrial pressure, producing the S1 sound.

questions.q: Which of the following is a clinical vignette — A 70-year-old man presents with breathlessness worse when lying flat, bilateral ankle oedema, and reduced exercise tolerance. His BNP is elevated. Which diagnosis fits best?
opts: A. Pulmonary embolism
B. Pneumonia with right-sided consolidation
C. Congestive cardiac failure
D. Aortic stenosis without heart failure
E. Atrial fibrillation without structural disease
questions.exp: This presentation — orthopnoea, peripheral oedema, and reduced exercise tolerance with elevated BNP — is classic congestive cardiac failure (CCF). BNP is released by ventricular myocytes under stretch/pressure and is a biomarker of heart failure.

questions.q: A patient's ECG shows no discernible P waves with an irregularly irregular rhythm. The most likely diagnosis is:
opts: A. First-degree AV block
B. Atrial fibrillation
C. Ventricular tachycardia
D. Sinus bradycardia
E. Complete heart block
questions.exp: Absent P waves with an irregularly irregular rhythm is the hallmark ECG finding in atrial fibrillation (AF). The SA node is not generating organised impulses; instead chaotic atrial activity is present. In complete heart block, P waves are present but dissociated from QRS complexes.

questions.q: Trabeculae carneae are found lining the walls of which cardiac chambers?
opts: A. Both atria
B. Left atrium only
C. Both ventricles
D. Right ventricle only
E. All four chambers
questions.exp: Trabeculae carneae are irregular ridges of cardiac muscle covered by endocardium that line the walls of both ventricles. The atria have pectinate muscles instead. The left ventricle also has trabeculae carneae but lacks a moderator band.

questions.q: The pulmonary circuit is best described as:
opts: A. Transporting oxygenated blood from the left heart to the body
B. Draining the myocardium via coronary veins
C. Pumping deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs and returning oxygenated blood to the left atrium
D. Supplying blood to the conducting system of the heart
E. Moving blood from the left ventricle through the aorta
questions.exp: The pulmonary circuit transports deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle via the pulmonary trunk and arteries to the lungs, where gas exchange occurs. Oxygenated blood then returns via four pulmonary veins to the left atrium to enter the systemic circuit.

questions.q: A 55-year-old woman has an echocardiogram showing a poorly contracting left ventricle. The ESPVR slope is flattened. This indicates:
opts: A. Good myocardial contractility
B. Increased ventricular compliance
C. Reduced myocardial contractility
D. Increased afterload
E. Normal systolic function
questions.exp: The ESPVR slope is a measure of myocardial contractility. A flattened (decreased) slope indicates reduced contractility — the ventricle cannot develop as much pressure for a given end-systolic volume. This is characteristic of systolic heart failure.

questions.q: Which of the following represents the correct sequence of the cardiac conduction system?
opts: A. AV node → SA node → Bundle of His → Purkinje fibres
B. SA node → AV node → Bundle of His → Bundle branches → Purkinje fibres
C. SA node → Bundle of His → AV node → Purkinje fibres
D. Purkinje fibres → Bundle branches → AV node → SA node
E. SA node → Purkinje fibres → AV node → Bundle of His
questions.exp: The correct sequence is: SA node (initiates impulse) → atrial myocardium → AV node (delays) → Bundle of His (penetrates septum) → right and left bundle branches → Purkinje fibre network (rapid ventricular activation). This sequence ensures coordinated atrial then ventricular contraction.

questions.q: Sympathetic stimulation of the SA node increases heart rate by which mechanism?
opts: A. Increasing the threshold potential required for action potentials
B. Reducing the rate of funny channel (If) current, slowing depolarisation
C. Increasing the rate of funny current (If), accelerating prepotential depolarisation toward threshold
D. Blocking L-type Ca2+ channels during the upstroke
E. Hyperpolarizing the cell to -80mV
questions.exp: Sympathetic stimulation increases cAMP, which enhances the funny current (If) — the SA node cells depolarise more rapidly toward threshold. This shortens the cycle length and increases heart rate. Parasympathetic (vagal) stimulation has the opposite effect.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the role of the Bundle of His?
opts: A. It initiates the cardiac impulse
B. It delays conduction between the atria and ventricles
C. It penetrates the membranous interventricular septum, connecting the AV node to the bundle branches
D. It distributes the impulse throughout the ventricular myocardium
E. It repolarizes the ventricles
questions.exp: The Bundle of His (AV bundle) penetrates the fibrous membranous septum after the AV node, forming the only electrical connection between the atria and ventricles (the cardiac skeleton electrically isolates them elsewhere). It then splits into the left and right bundle branches.

questions.q: In a complete AV block, which structure acts as the tertiary pacemaker to prevent cardiac arrest?
opts: A. SA node
B. AV node
C. Purkinje fibres
D. Atrial myocardium
E. Moderator band
questions.exp: In complete AV block (where no impulses pass from atria to ventricles), the Purkinje fibres act as the tertiary pacemaker at 15–40 bpm, generating a ventricular escape rhythm to prevent cardiac arrest. This is a slow, broad-complex rhythm.

questions.q: A patient's ECG shows ST-segment changes in leads V3 and V4. These chest leads are best used for detecting abnormalities in which territory?
opts: A. Inferior wall supplied by the right coronary artery
B. High lateral wall supplied by the circumflex artery
C. Anterior wall / septal territory supplied by the LAD
D. Posterior wall
E. Right ventricle
questions.exp: V3–V4 overlie the anterior and septal walls of the heart, primarily supplied by the left anterior descending (LAD) artery. ST changes in these leads suggest anterior/septal ischaemia or infarction. V1–V2 are septal; V5–V6 are lateral.

questions.q: Phospholamban regulates SERCA activity. In heart failure, reduced SERCA activity would impair which process?
opts: A. L-type Ca2+ channel opening
B. Ca2+-induced Ca2+ release from the SR
C. Pumping Ca2+ back into the SR during relaxation, impairing diastolic function
D. Na+/Ca2+ exchange across the sarcolemma
E. Action potential conduction through gap junctions
questions.exp: SERCA (SR Ca2+-ATPase) pumps Ca2+ back into the SR during relaxation. Reduced SERCA activity (as seen in heart failure) means Ca2+ is not efficiently sequestered, leading to elevated cytosolic Ca2+, impaired relaxation (diastolic dysfunction), and reduced SR Ca2+ available for the next contraction.

questions.q: During the Wiggers diagram, the mitral valve closes at which point?
opts: A. At the beginning of rapid ventricular filling
B. When atrial pressure falls below ventricular pressure at the start of isovolumetric contraction
C. When ventricular pressure exceeds aortic pressure
D. At the end of isovolumetric relaxation
E. During the dicrotic notch
questions.exp: The mitral valve closes at the end of diastole (beginning of isovolumetric contraction) when rising ventricular pressure exceeds left atrial pressure, producing the S1 heart sound. It opens again at the end of isovolumetric relaxation when ventricular pressure drops below atrial pressure.

questions.q: Which of the following aortic sinus gives rise to no coronary artery?
opts: A. Left posterior aortic sinus
B. Anterior aortic sinus
C. Right posterior aortic sinus
D. Both posterior sinuses
E. All three sinuses give rise to coronary arteries
questions.exp: There are three dilations (aortic sinuses) just superior to the aortic semilunar valve. The left posterior aortic sinus gives rise to the left coronary artery; the anterior sinus gives rise to the right coronary artery; the right posterior sinus gives rise to no vessel.

pathways.name: Hypoxia Cascade
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the cellular cascade triggered by oxygen deprivation
steps: Low oxygen (hypoxia)
Electron Transport Chain disrupted
ATP production fails
Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump disrupted
Less ions pumped out of cell
Cell swells with water (cytotoxic oedema)
Calcium floods into the cell
Decreased mitochondrial function → cell death

pathways.name: Insulin Release
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the events leading to insulin secretion from pancreatic beta cells
steps: Blood glucose rises above 4.4–5.0 mmol/L
Glucose enters beta cells via GLUT transporters
Glucose metabolised → ATP produced
ATP closes ATP-gated K⁺ channels
K⁺ cannot leave → membrane depolarises
Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels open
Ca²⁺ floods into beta cell
Insulin granule exocytosis

pathways.name: Hypoglycaemia Defence
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the four lines of defence against falling blood glucose in order
steps: Decrease insulin secretion (first defence)
Increase glucagon secretion (hormonal defence)
Increase epinephrine (backup for glucagon)
Increase cortisol and growth hormone (slow, sustained recovery)

pathways.name: Beta-Oxidation Entry
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of fatty acid mobilisation and mitochondrial entry
steps: Triglycerides hydrolysed → fatty acids + glycerol (cytoplasm)
Fatty acid activated → Fatty acyl-CoA (cytoplasm)
Fatty acyl-CoA + Carnitine → Fatty acyl-carnitine
Fatty acyl-carnitine crosses inner mitochondrial membrane
Fatty acyl-carnitine → Fatty acyl-CoA (mitochondrial matrix)
Beta-oxidation: FAD reductions + hydration → Beta-ketoacyl-CoA
Beta-ketoacyl-CoA split → Acetyl-CoA + shorter Acyl-CoA
Acetyl-CoA enters Krebs cycle

pathways.name: CO2 Transport in Blood
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of CO2 transport from peripheral tissue to exhalation in the correct order
steps: CO2 produced by cellular respiration in peripheral tissues
CO2 diffuses from tissue into plasma and erythrocytes
In RBC: carbonic anhydrase converts CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 → H+ + HCO3−
HCO3− diffuses out of RBC into plasma (chloride shifts in to maintain neutrality)
CO2 also binds to Hb globin chains forming carbaminohaemoglobin (20%)
Venous blood (containing dissolved CO2, HCO3−, HbCO2) travels to pulmonary capillaries
In lungs: O2 binds Hb (Haldane effect) → CO2 released from Hb
Carbonic anhydrase reverses reaction: HCO3− pulled back into RBC, CO2 regenerated
Chloride shifts OUT of RBC back into plasma
CO2 diffuses across respiratory membrane into alveolus and is exhaled

pathways.name: O2 Transport — Lungs to Tissues
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of oxygen transport from the atmosphere to peripheral tissue mitochondria
steps: O2 inhaled into alveoli (PAO2 ~104 mmHg)
O2 diffuses across respiratory membrane into pulmonary capillary blood (PO2 gradient: 104 → 40 mmHg)
O2 binds to haemoglobin (T→R conformational change, cooperative binding) forming HbO2
Oxygenated blood returned to left heart via pulmonary veins
Left ventricle pumps oxygenated blood into systemic circulation
Blood arrives at tissue capillaries (tissue PO2 ~40 mmHg, blood PO2 ~100 mmHg)
High H+, CO2, temperature in tissues causes Bohr effect (R→T shift, reduced O2 affinity)
O2 dissociates from haemoglobin and diffuses into interstitial fluid
O2 diffuses into cells and to mitochondria for oxidative phosphorylation
CO2 produced as metabolic byproduct and begins transport back to lungs

pathways.name: Respiratory Exchange Membrane
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the layers of the respiratory exchange membrane from alveolar lumen to capillary lumen
steps: Alveolar lumen (air space) with O2 and CO2
Surfactant layer (produced by Type II pneumocytes — reduces surface tension)
Type I pneumocyte (squamous alveolar epithelium — 95% of surface area)
Shared basal membrane (connective tissue, collagen, fluid)
Capillary endothelium (simple squamous epithelium)
Capillary lumen containing erythrocytes and plasma

pathways.name: Chloride Shift (at tissues)
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of the chloride shift occurring at peripheral tissue capillaries
steps: CO2 diffuses from tissues into erythrocytes
Carbonic anhydrase catalyses: CO2 + H2O → H2CO3
H2CO3 dissociates to H+ + HCO3−
H+ binds to haemoglobin (buffering), contributing to Bohr effect
HCO3− accumulates inside the RBC creating a concentration gradient
HCO3− diffuses out of RBC into plasma via band 3 protein antiporter
Cl− (chloride) shifts INTO the RBC to maintain electrical neutrality
HCO3− distributed throughout plasma volume, increasing CO2 carrying capacity

pathways.name: V/Q Mismatch — Physiological Response
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the physiological steps that occur in response to a localised V/Q mismatch (low V/Q area)
steps: Area of lung becomes poorly ventilated (e.g. mucus plug, bronchoconstriction)
Alveolar CO2 rises and O2 falls in the affected area (low V/Q)
Local hypoxia causes pulmonary arteriolar vasoconstriction (hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction)
Blood flow is redirected away from the poorly ventilated alveoli
CO2 accumulation in airways causes local bronchodilation
Airflow is preferentially directed towards better-ventilated alveoli
V/Q ratio is improved in the affected area, reducing hypoxaemia
If compensation fails, systemic hypoxaemia and elevated A-a gradient result

pathways.name: Air Flow — Nose to Alveolus
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the structures air passes through from the external environment to the alveolus, in the correct order
steps: External environment (atmospheric air at 760 mmHg)
Nares (nostrils) — air enters through alar cartilage-bounded openings
Nasal cavity — warmed, humidified, filtered by conchae and meatuses
Nasopharynx — lined with respiratory epithelium, rigid non-collapsible walls
Oropharynx — lined with stratified squamous epithelium
Laryngopharynx — passes the epiglottis (which covers trachea during swallowing)
Larynx — through the glottis (between vocal cords) into the trachea
Trachea — C-shaped hyaline cartilage rings, pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium
Primary bronchi — branch at the carina, enter lungs at hilum
Secondary bronchi → tertiary bronchioles → terminal bronchioles (conducting zone ends)
Respiratory bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveolar sacs
Alveoli — gas exchange across the respiratory membrane

pathways.name: Mechanics of Inspiration
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the sequence of events that occur during a normal quiet inspiration
steps: Medulla oblongata (VRG) sends efferent signals to respiratory muscles
Diaphragm contracts and descends (accounts for 80% of inspiratory effort)
External intercostal muscles contract, elevating the ribs
Thoracic cavity volume increases in all dimensions
Intrapleural pressure becomes more negative (from −4 to approx −8 mmHg)
Pleural fluid surface tension couples lung to chest wall — lung expands with thorax
Intra-alveolar pressure falls below atmospheric (760 mmHg)
Pressure gradient drives air into the lungs down the pressure gradient
Tidal volume of air (~500 mL) enters conducting zone, then reaches alveoli
Gas exchange occurs at the alveoli; O2 enters blood, CO2 exits

pathways.name: Laryngeal Cartilages — Hierarchy
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the laryngeal cartilages from superior to inferior, in anatomical order
steps: Epiglottis — elastic cartilage, most superior, hinged flap covering laryngeal inlet
Thyroid cartilage — largest cartilage, forms Adam's apple (laryngeal prominence)
Arytenoid cartilages (paired) — sit atop the cricoid, attach vocal cords, enable speech
Corniculate cartilages — apex of arytenoids, anchor epiglottis via aryepiglottic folds
Cuneiform cartilages — within aryepiglottic folds, support the fold structure
Cricoid cartilage — inferior and complete ring, forms base of larynx, junction with trachea

pathways.name: Pharynx — Superior to Inferior
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the regions of the pharynx from superior to inferior with their key features
steps: Nasopharynx — posterior to nasal cavity, above soft palate, rigid walls, respiratory epithelium
Adenoids (pharyngeal tonsils) and Waldeyer's ring — lymphoid tissue in nasopharynx roof
Orifice of auditory (Eustachian) tube — opens in lateral nasopharynx, connects to middle ear
Oropharynx — posterior to oral cavity, between soft palate and epiglottis, stratified squamous epithelium
Fauces — connection between mouth and oropharynx, palatine tonsils in tonsillar fossae
Laryngopharynx — from tip of epiglottis to C6, piriform fossae laterally, arytenoids anteriorly
Termination at C6/cricoid level — laryngopharynx becomes oesophagus posteriorly

pathways.name: Spirometry Interpretation — Step by Step
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of systematically interpreting a spirometry result
steps: Check technical quality — was the effort adequate? (sharp peak on flow-volume curve, plateau on volume-time)
Calculate FEV1/FVC ratio (FER) from the reported values
Determine pattern: FER <0.7 = obstructive; FER >0.8 = restrictive; 0.7-0.8 = borderline
If obstructive: assess severity by % predicted FEV1 (mild >80%, moderate 50-79%, severe <50%)
If restrictive: assess FVC % predicted (and ideally TLC if available to confirm restriction)
Check for bronchodilator reversibility: if FEV1 improves >12% and >200mL post-bronchodilator = significant reversibility (asthma)
Correlate with clinical picture and symptoms to reach a diagnosis

pathways.name: Primary Haemostasis
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of primary haemostasis in the correct order following vessel injury
steps: Vessel injury exposes subendothelial collagen and elastin
Von Willebrand Factor (VWF) bridges platelets to exposed collagen via GpIb receptor (Adhesion)
Adherent platelets become activated and change shape
Activated platelets release granule contents: ADP, Thromboxane A2 (Activation)
ADP and TXA2 recruit additional platelets to the site
Activated platelets bind to each other via fibrinogen bridging GpIIb/IIIa receptors (Aggregation)
Primary haemostatic plug formed

pathways.name: Coagulation Cascade
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the key steps of the coagulation cascade from vessel injury to stable clot
steps: Vessel injury exposes Tissue Factor (TF) from perivascular cells
TF binds Factor VIIa → TF-FVIIa complex formed (Extrinsic pathway initiated)
TF-FVIIa complex activates Factors IX and X
Simultaneously: intrinsic pathway activates XII→XI→IX→X (Contact activation)
Factor Xa + cofactor Va → Prothrombinase complex formed (Common pathway)
Prothrombinase complex converts Prothrombin (II) → Thrombin (IIa)
Thrombin cleaves Fibrinogen → Fibrin monomers
Fibrin monomers polymerise into fibrin strands
Factor XIIIa (activated by thrombin) cross-links fibrin → Stable fibrin mesh (clot)

pathways.name: Erythropoiesis
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the stages of red blood cell development from stem cell to mature erythrocyte
steps: Haematopoietic stem cell (Haemocytoblast) in red bone marrow
Stem cell differentiates into Common Myeloid Progenitor (CMP)
CMP commits to the erythroid lineage → Proerythroblast
Proerythroblast: haemoglobin synthesis begins; cell has nucleus; divides to make more cells
Erythroblast (Normoblast): cells divide and produce large amounts of haemoglobin
Reticulocyte: immature RBC loses its nucleus and enters the bloodstream
Erythrocyte: mature red blood cell capable of carrying oxygen (120-day lifespan)

pathways.name: Stopping Coagulation
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the three anticoagulant systems in order of the phase of coagulation they target
steps: Tissue Factor Pathway Inhibitor (TFPI) — inhibits the initiation phase by blocking the TF-FVIIa complex
Thrombin generated → binds Thrombomodulin on healthy endothelium
Thrombomodulin-thrombin complex activates Protein C
Activated Protein C inactivates Factors Va and VIIIa — dampens amplification phase
Antithrombin directly inhibits thrombin, Xa, IXa, XIa — controls propagation phase
Fibrinolysis: Plasminogen → Plasmin (via tPA)
Plasmin cuts fibrin threads → Fibrin degradation products removed in urine

pathways.name: Cardiac Output
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the key steps and factors in determining cardiac output from cellular signalling to final output
steps: Sympathetic stimulation releases norepinephrine onto SA node
NE binds beta-1 adrenergic receptors on SA node
Na+ and Ca2+ channels open, increasing depolarisation rate
Heart rate (HR) increases
Simultaneously, increased contractility increases ventricular force
End Diastolic Volume (EDV) measured at end of filling
End Systolic Volume (ESV) measured after ventricular ejection
Stroke Volume calculated: SV = EDV − ESV
Cardiac Output calculated: CO = HR × SV
CO compared to demand; cardiovascular centre adjusts autonomic tone

pathways.name: Baroreceptor Reflex
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of the baroreceptor reflex in response to a sudden rise in blood pressure
steps: Blood pressure rises (e.g. from increased cardiac output)
Increased stretch detected by baroreceptors in aortic sinus and carotid bodies
Baroreceptors increase firing rate
Signals travel via vagus and glossopharyngeal nerves to medulla oblongata
Cardioinhibitory centre activated; cardioaccelerator centre suppressed
Parasympathetic (vagal) output to SA node increases
Acetylcholine released at SA node; K+ channels open
Heart rate decreases
Stroke volume decreases
Blood pressure returns toward homeostatic level

pathways.name: Capillary Exchange
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps describing the Starling forces and fluid movement across a capillary from arterial to venous end
steps: Blood enters capillary at arterial end with CHP ~35 mmHg
BCOP (~26 mmHg) opposes outward movement due to plasma proteins
Net filtration pressure = CHP − BCOP = +9 mmHg at arterial end
Fluid filters out of capillary into interstitial space (filtration)
Mid-capillary: CHP falls to ~25 mmHg, equals BCOP — no net movement
At venous end: CHP falls to ~18 mmHg, below BCOP of 25 mmHg
Net filtration pressure is negative (−7 mmHg) — reabsorption occurs
Fluid moves from interstitium back into capillary at venous end
Excess fluid not reabsorbed (~3 L/day) enters lymphatic capillaries
Lymph is returned to circulation via thoracic duct and subclavian vein

pathways.name: Blood Vessel Structure
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the layers of a blood vessel wall from innermost to outermost, including key sub-components
steps: Lumen — hollow centre through which blood flows
Endothelium — simple squamous epithelium lining the tunica intima
Basement membrane (basal lamina) — binds endothelium to connective tissue
Areolar connective tissue and elastic fibres — outer tunica intima
Internal elastic membrane — boundary between tunica intima and tunica media (large arteries only)
Tunica media — smooth muscle and elastic fibres in circular sheets
External elastic membrane — separates tunica media from tunica externa (large arteries only)
Tunica externa — collagenous and elastic fibres providing outer structural support
Vasa vasorum — tiny vessels nourishing the vessel wall within tunica externa
Nervi vasorum — nerve fibres controlling smooth muscle contraction in vessel walls

pathways.name: Lymphatic Drainage
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the pathway of lymph from the interstitial fluid back to the bloodstream
steps: Excess interstitial fluid accumulates as filtration exceeds reabsorption
Interstitial fluid enters blind-ending lymphatic capillaries via overlapping endothelial flaps
Lymphatic capillaries merge into larger lymphatic vessels (similar structure to veins)
Lymph passes through lymph nodes for immune filtration
Lymphatic vessels converge into lymphatic trunks
Left side of body and lower limbs drain into the thoracic duct
Right upper limb, right head and thorax drain into the right lymphatic duct
Thoracic duct originates at the cisterna chyli, below the diaphragm
Thoracic duct ascends and drains into the left subclavian vein
Right lymphatic duct drains into the right subclavian vein — lymph re-enters bloodstream

pathways.name: Vessel Hierarchy
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the blood vessels in order from the heart through the systemic circuit and back
steps: Left ventricle contracts — blood enters aorta (elastic artery)
Blood flows into muscular arteries (distributing arteries)
Muscular arteries branch into arterioles (resistance vessels)
Arterioles feed metarterioles with precapillary sphincters
Precapillary sphincters open — blood enters capillary bed
Gas and nutrient exchange occurs across capillary walls (microcirculation)
Blood exits capillaries into postcapillary venules
Venules merge to form veins
Veins drain into the venae cavae (superior and inferior)
Blood returns to the right atrium of the heart

pathways.name: Cardiac Conduction
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of the cardiac conduction pathway in the correct order
steps: SA node fires — initiates impulse at junction of superior vena cava and right atrium
Impulse spreads across atrial myocardium — both atria depolarize and contract
Impulse arrives at the AV node — deliberate 0.09s delay allows ventricular filling
Impulse passes through the Bundle of His — penetrates the membranous interventricular septum
Impulse travels down the right and left bundle branches (left branch splits into anterior and posterior)
Purkinje fibre network rapidly distributes the impulse throughout the ventricular myocardium
Ventricles contract simultaneously in a coordinated fashion — systole

pathways.name: Cardiac Cycle
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the four major phases of the cardiac cycle in the correct order
steps: Ventricular filling (Diastole) — mitral valve opens, rapid passive filling, then diastasis, then atrial kick (15–25% of filling)
Isovolumetric contraction — mitral valve closes (S1), all valves shut, ventricular pressure rises rapidly at constant volume
Ventricular ejection (Systole) — aortic valve opens when LV pressure exceeds aortic pressure; blood ejected into aorta; ventricular volume falls
Isovolumetric relaxation — aortic valve closes (S2, dicrotic notch); all valves shut; ventricular pressure falls rapidly at constant volume until mitral valve reopens

pathways.name: Pulmonary Circuit
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of blood flow through the pulmonary circuit in the correct order
steps: Deoxygenated blood returns from the body via the superior and inferior vena cava
Blood enters the right atrium
Blood passes through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle
Right ventricle contracts — blood ejected through the pulmonary semilunar valve
Blood enters the pulmonary trunk, which bifurcates into left and right pulmonary arteries
Blood flows into pulmonary capillaries surrounding the alveoli — gas exchange occurs (CO2 out, O2 in)
Oxygenated blood returns to the left atrium via four pulmonary veins
Blood passes through the mitral (bicuspid) valve into the left ventricle
Left ventricle contracts — blood ejected through the aortic semilunar valve into the aorta
Oxygenated blood distributed to the systemic circulation

pathways.name: E-C Coupling
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of excitation-contraction coupling in cardiac muscle in the correct order
steps: Action potential generated in cardiac contractile cell
Action potential travels down T-tubules into the cell interior
L-type (voltage-gated) Ca2+ channels open — small Ca2+ influx from extracellular fluid
Small Ca2+ influx triggers Ca2+-induced Ca2+ release (CICR) from the sarcoplasmic reticulum
Cytosolic Ca2+ rises sharply
Ca2+ binds to troponin C — troponin-tropomyosin complex shifts, exposing actin binding sites
Cross-bridge cycling begins — myosin heads bind actin, thin filaments slide inward between thick filaments
Contraction occurs — sarcomere shortens
L-type Ca2+ channels close at end of plateau phase — Ca2+ entry from ECF ceases
SERCA pumps Ca2+ back into the SR; NCX removes Ca2+ to ECF — cytosolic Ca2+ falls below troponin threshold
Troponin-tropomyosin complex returns to resting position — myofilaments dissociate — relaxation

pathways.name: Glycolysis
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the 10 steps of glycolysis in the correct order from glucose to pyruvate
steps: Glucose → Glucose-6-phosphate (hexokinase; consumes 1 ATP)
Glucose-6-phosphate → Fructose-6-phosphate (phosphoglucose isomerase)
Fructose-6-phosphate → Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate (PFK-1; consumes 1 ATP — rate-limiting step)
Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate → DHAP + Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (aldolase)
DHAP → Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (triose phosphate isomerase; net ×2 from here)
G3P → 1,3-Bisphosphoglycerate (G3P dehydrogenase; produces NADH)
1,3-BPG → 3-Phosphoglycerate (phosphoglycerate kinase; produces ATP ×2)
3-Phosphoglycerate → 2-Phosphoglycerate (phosphoglycerate mutase)
2-Phosphoglycerate → Phosphoenolpyruvate (enolase; releases H2O)
Phosphoenolpyruvate → Pyruvate (pyruvate kinase; produces ATP ×2)

pathways.name: Krebs Cycle
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the 8 steps of the Krebs cycle from acetyl-CoA entry to oxaloacetate regeneration
steps: Acetyl-CoA + Oxaloacetate → Citrate (citrate synthase; step 1)
Citrate → Isocitrate (aconitase; step 2 — isomerisation)
Isocitrate → Alpha-ketoglutarate (isocitrate dehydrogenase; step 3 — releases CO2, produces NADH)
Alpha-ketoglutarate → Succinyl-CoA (alpha-KG dehydrogenase; step 4 — releases CO2, produces NADH)
Succinyl-CoA → Succinate (succinyl-CoA synthetase; step 5 — produces GTP)
Succinate → Fumarate (succinate dehydrogenase; step 6 — produces FADH2)
Fumarate → Malate (fumarase; step 7 — adds H2O)
Malate → Oxaloacetate (malate dehydrogenase; step 8 — produces NADH; cycle complete)

pathways.name: Electron Transport Chain
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the sequence of electron flow through the ETC leading to ATP synthesis
steps: NADH donates electrons to Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase); H+ pumped to intermembrane space
FADH2 donates electrons to Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase); no H+ pumped at this step
Electrons transferred from Complexes I and II to Ubiquinone (mobile electron carrier)
Ubiquinol transfers electrons to Complex III (cytochrome bc1); H+ pumped
Electrons transferred from Complex III to Cytochrome c (mobile electron carrier)
Cytochrome c transfers electrons to Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase); H+ pumped
At Complex IV: electrons + O2 + H+ → H2O (oxygen is terminal electron acceptor)
H+ gradient established across inner mitochondrial membrane (high in intermembrane space)
H+ flows back into matrix through ATP synthase (Complex V) — rotational mechanism
ATP synthase phosphorylates ADP + Pi → ATP; ~3 ATP per NADH, ~2 per FADH2

pathways.name: Gluconeogenesis
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the key steps of gluconeogenesis from pyruvate to free glucose
steps: Pyruvate + CO2 + ATP → Oxaloacetate (pyruvate carboxylase; mitochondria)
Oxaloacetate → Malate for transport across inner mitochondrial membrane
Malate → Oxaloacetate (cytoplasm) — regenerated for next step
Oxaloacetate + GTP → Phosphoenolpyruvate + CO2 (PEPCK; bypasses pyruvate kinase)
PEP ← 2-PG ← 3-PG ← 1,3-BPG ← G3P (reversal of glycolytic steps 9→6)
G3P + DHAP → Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate (aldolase — reverse of glycolytic step 4)
Fructose-1,6-bisphosphate → Fructose-6-phosphate (fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase; bypasses PFK-1)
Fructose-6-phosphate → Glucose-6-phosphate (phosphoglucose isomerase)
Glucose-6-phosphate → Free Glucose + Pi (glucose-6-phosphatase; bypasses hexokinase — liver/kidney only)
Glucose released into bloodstream via GLUT-2 transporters

pathways.name: Ketogenesis
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of ketone body production from excess acetyl-CoA
steps: Prolonged fasting or uncontrolled diabetes — low insulin, high glucagon
Lipolysis releases fatty acids from adipose tissue
Fatty acids enter hepatic mitochondria via carnitine shuttle
Beta-oxidation produces excess acetyl-CoA, overloading the Krebs cycle
2 × Acetyl-CoA → Acetoacetyl-CoA (thiolase)
Acetoacetyl-CoA + Acetyl-CoA → HMG-CoA (HMG-CoA synthase)
HMG-CoA → Acetoacetate + Acetyl-CoA (HMG-CoA lyase)
Acetoacetate reduced to Beta-hydroxybutyrate (primary ketone in blood)
Acetoacetate spontaneously decarboxylates to Acetone (exhaled — sweet breath in DKA)
Ketone bodies transported to peripheral tissues as alternative fuel for brain, heart, and muscle

pathways.name: Glucose Fate — Fed State
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the fates of dietary glucose in the fed state from absorption to storage
steps: Dietary carbohydrates digested to monosaccharides by salivary and pancreatic amylase
Monosaccharides absorbed by intestinal epithelium and enter portal circulation
Blood glucose rises → pancreatic beta cells detect rise above 4.4–5.0 mmol/L
Insulin secreted → promotes GLUT-4 insertion in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue
Skeletal muscle takes up glucose (primary site) → glycolysis or glycogenesis
Liver takes up excess glucose via GLUT-2 → glycogenesis (up to ~75g stored)
When glycogen stores full → excess glucose converted to acetyl-CoA → fatty acid synthesis
Fatty acids packaged as VLDL → transported to adipose tissue for triglyceride storage
Insulin simultaneously suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis
Blood glucose returns to fasting level (~4.4–5.0 mmol/L)

pathways.name: Respiratory Control Centre
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the components of respiratory control from pacemaker rhythm generation to breathing output
steps: Pre-Bötzinger complex (pacemaker cells) generates basic respiratory rhythm
Dorsal Respiratory Group (DRG) integrates afferent input from stretch receptors and peripheral chemoreceptors
Ventral Respiratory Group (VRG) coordinates expiratory muscles for forced breathing
Apneustic centre (lower pons) stimulates sustained deep inspiration
Pneumotaxic centre (upper pons) inhibits DRG to limit inspiration and regulate rate/depth
Central chemoreceptors (medulla) detect rising CO2 crossing the blood-brain barrier → increasing H+ in CSF
Peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid and aortic bodies) detect falling PaO2 below ~60 mmHg
Hering-Breuer reflex: lung stretch receptors fire when overinflated → vagal signal limits further inspiration
Efferent signals travel via phrenic nerve (C3-5) and intercostal nerves to respiratory muscles
Coordinated contraction of diaphragm and intercostals drives ventilation

pathways.name: O2 Dissociation — Right Shift
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the sequence of events causing a right shift of the O2 dissociation curve at working tissues
steps: Active tissue produces CO2, H+, lactic acid, and heat
CO2 diffuses into erythrocytes → carbonic anhydrase → H+ produced
Increased H+ binds to haemoglobin globin chains
H+ binding stabilises the T (taut) state — haemoglobin's low-affinity conformation
Increased temperature further destabilises the haem-O2 bond
2,3-DPG (elevated in chronic hypoxia or anaemia) binds deoxyhemoglobin, further stabilising T state
O2 dissociation curve shifts right — lower O2 affinity at any given PO2
More O2 unloaded to tissues for the same arterial PO2
CO2 transported back to lungs (Bohr effect maximising O2 delivery)
At pulmonary capillaries: O2 binds → Haldane effect → CO2 released → pH rises → left shift restores affinity

pathways.name: Haemoglobin — Cooperative Binding
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of cooperative oxygen binding to haemoglobin from T state to fully loaded R state
steps: Deoxyhaemoglobin exists in T (taut) state — all 4 subunits in low-affinity conformation
First O2 molecule binds to an alpha or beta haem iron (Fe2+)
O2 binding causes conformational change in that subunit — local shift toward R state
Conformational change transmitted to other subunits via intersubunit contacts
Second O2 binds more easily — affinity has increased (cooperativity)
Third O2 binds with even higher affinity
Fourth O2 binds — haemoglobin now fully in R (relaxed) state — high affinity
Sigmoid O2 dissociation curve results from this cooperative mechanism
At tissues: first O2 released → R→T shift → facilitates further unloading of remaining O2
Full unloading at tissue PO2 ~40 mmHg releases approximately 25% of carried O2

pathways.name: Mechanics of Expiration
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the sequence of events during normal quiet expiration
steps: Neural inspiration signal ceases — diaphragm and external intercostals relax
Elastic recoil of stretched lung tissue begins
Lung volume decreases
Intra-alveolar pressure rises above atmospheric pressure (760 mmHg)
Pressure gradient reverses — air flows out of lungs
Approximately 500 mL of tidal air expelled
Intrapleural pressure returns toward −4 mmHg at rest
Lungs return to functional residual capacity (FRC) — the resting lung volume

pathways.name: Surfactant — Production to Function
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of surfactant production, secretion, and physiological function
steps: Type II pneumocytes synthesise dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC) — the main surfactant component
Surfactant stored in lamellar bodies within Type II pneumocytes
Surfactant secreted by exocytosis onto the alveolar surface
Surfactant molecules form a monomolecular layer at the air-fluid interface
Surfactant reduces surface tension of alveolar fluid by disrupting hydrogen bonds between water molecules
Lower surface tension reduces the tendency for alveoli to collapse (Laplace's law: P = 2T/r)
Small alveoli do not collapse — surfactant effect is proportionally greater at smaller radii
Prevents atelectasis and reduces the work of breathing
Surfactant deficiency (e.g. premature neonates) leads to alveolar collapse and hypoxaemia (IRDS)

pathways.name: Mucociliary Escalator
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of mucociliary clearance from particle deposition to removal
steps: Inhaled particles (bacteria, dust, pollen) deposit on mucus lining of conducting airways
Goblet cells and submucosal glands secrete mucus (gel layer) over the periciliary fluid (sol layer)
Cilia of pseudostratified columnar epithelium beat in coordinated metachronal waves
Cilia beat upward (toward pharynx) at ~10–20 beats/second
Mucus and trapped particles transported superiorly along the airway
Mucus escalated up through trachea to larynx
At larynx: mucus either swallowed or expelled by coughing
Ciliary dysfunction (e.g. Kartagener's syndrome — dynein arm defect) impairs clearance → recurrent infection

pathways.name: Platelet Activation — Intracellular
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the intracellular events within an activating platelet from receptor binding to aggregation
steps: ADP or TXA2 binds platelet surface receptors (P2Y12 or TP receptors)
Receptor activation coupled to Gq protein → activates phospholipase C
PLC cleaves PIP2 → IP3 + DAG
IP3 releases Ca2+ from dense tubular system (platelet equivalent of SR)
DAG activates protein kinase C
Ca2+ activates myosin light-chain kinase → platelet shape change (sphere with pseudopods)
Ca2+ triggers dense granule release (ADP, serotonin) and alpha granule release (vWF, fibrinogen)
Arachidonic acid released from membrane → COX-1 → TXA2 synthesised (amplification loop)
GpIIb/IIIa undergoes conformational activation → binds fibrinogen bridges between platelets
Platelet aggregation complete — primary haemostatic plug formed

pathways.name: Haemoglobin Synthesis
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the key steps in haemoglobin synthesis from precursors to functional tetramer
steps: Glycine + Succinyl-CoA → Delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA synthase; rate-limiting step; requires vitamin B6)
ALA → Porphobilinogen (ALA dehydratase; inhibited by lead)
4 × Porphobilinogen → Hydroxymethylbilane → Uroporphyrinogen III
Uroporphyrinogen III → Coproporphyrinogen III → Protoporphyrin IX
Protoporphyrin IX + Fe2+ → Haem (ferrochelatase; inhibited by lead)
Globin chains (alpha and beta) synthesised separately on ribosomes
Haem inserted into hydrophobic pocket of each globin chain
4 haem-globin subunits assemble into the functional tetramer (2 alpha + 2 beta = HbA)
Haemoglobin packaged in developing erythrocytes during erythropoiesis

pathways.name: Bainbridge Reflex
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the steps of the Bainbridge (atrial) reflex in response to increased venous return
steps: Increased venous return causes elevated right atrial pressure
Specialised stretch baroreceptors in the right atrial wall are activated
Increased baroreceptor firing rate transmitted via vagal afferents to the medulla
Cardiovascular centre interprets signal: atria are overfull — needs faster ejection
Cardioinhibitory centre suppresses parasympathetic (vagal) output to SA node
Cardioaccelerator centre increases sympathetic drive to SA node
Heart rate increases — blood pumped out of the heart more rapidly
Increased stroke volume and CO clear the excess atrial volume
Atrial pressure normalises → baroreceptor firing returns to baseline

pathways.name: Haemorrhage Response
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the cardiovascular compensatory responses to acute haemorrhage in correct order
steps: Acute blood loss → reduced circulating volume → reduced venous return
Reduced venous return → reduced cardiac output and blood pressure
Baroreceptors in aortic sinus and carotid bodies sense decreased stretch → reduced firing
Cardiovascular centre interprets low firing as low blood pressure
Increased sympathetic outflow: increased HR and increased contractility
Arteriolar vasoconstriction → increased peripheral vascular resistance
Venoconstriction → autotransfusion from venous capacitance vessels
Adrenal medulla releases epinephrine and norepinephrine — sustains sympathetic response
Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system activated → fluid and sodium retention
ADH (vasopressin) released → water retention and vasoconstriction

pathways.name: SA Node Action Potential
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the phases of the SA node pacemaker action potential from maximum diastolic potential to threshold
steps: Phase 4 begins: Maximum diastolic potential (~−65 mV) — cell is hyperpolarised after previous AP
Funny current (If) channels open: Na+ and K+ slowly depolarise the cell (pacemaker potential)
T-type Ca2+ channels open as voltage reaches ~−55 mV — accelerating depolarisation
Threshold (~−40 mV) reached
Phase 0 upstroke: L-type Ca2+ channels open — rapid Ca2+ influx drives upstroke (not Na+ as in other cells)
Peak of action potential (~+10 to +20 mV)
Phase 3 repolarisation: K+ channels (IKr, IKs) open → K+ efflux repolarises the cell
Return to maximum diastolic potential — cycle repeats automatically at intrinsic rate ~100 bpm

pathways.name: Ventricular Action Potential
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the five phases of the ventricular cardiomyocyte action potential in order
steps: Phase 4 (resting): membrane at −90 mV; IK1 K+ channels maintain resting potential
Phase 0 (rapid depolarisation): voltage-gated Na+ channels open → rapid Na+ influx; upstroke to +30 mV
Phase 1 (early repolarisation): Na+ channels inactivate; transient K+ efflux (Ito) causes brief dip
Phase 2 (plateau): L-type Ca2+ channels open → Ca2+ influx balanced by K+ efflux; plateau ~0 mV
Phase 2 plateau: Ca2+ triggers CICR from SR → contraction occurs during this phase
Phase 3 (repolarisation): Ca2+ channels close; IKr and IKs K+ channels open → rapid K+ efflux → return to −90 mV
Absolute refractory period spans phases 0–2: no new AP possible (prevents tetanus in cardiac muscle)
Relative refractory period in late phase 3: new AP possible with stronger-than-normal stimulus

pathways.name: Coronary Circulation
pathways.subtitle: Arrange the coronary arteries and their territories from origin to distribution
steps: Aorta — right and left coronary arteries arise from the aortic sinuses just above the aortic valve
Left main coronary artery (LMCA) — short trunk arising from left posterior aortic sinus
LMCA bifurcates into Left Anterior Descending (LAD) and Circumflex arteries
LAD follows anterior interventricular sulcus — supplies anterior LV, anterior 2/3 of septum, and apex
Circumflex artery follows left coronary sulcus — supplies left atrium and lateral/posterior LV
Right coronary artery (RCA) — arises from anterior aortic sinus, follows right coronary sulcus
RCA gives marginal branch — supplies right ventricle lateral wall
RCA continues to posterior interventricular sulcus — gives posterior interventricular artery (right-dominant in 85%)
RCA supplies SA node (60%) and AV node (90%) — important territory in inferior MI
Cardiac veins (great, middle, small) drain into coronary sinus → empties into right atrium
